name: LL Akkurat
classification_notes: A Swiss neo-modernist grotesque with static construction, positioned in the lineage of neutral European sans-serifs. Lineto themselves describe it as embodying technical precision and neutrality in the tradition of Swiss graphic values.
summary: A Swiss neo-modernist grotesque by Laurenz Brunner, first released in 2004, that helped revive interest in neutral, precision-driven sans-serifs in contemporary type design.
description: Drawn while Brunner was studying at Central Saint Martins and published by Lineto in April 2004, LL Akkurat has since expanded from three weights to five, with a companion monospaced family and italics. The typeface's reach was extended substantially in 2019 with six new scripts: Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Arabic, Devanagari and Vietnamese, each developed by specialist designers under Brunner's direction. Its static construction and deliberate refusal of trend or elegance have made it a durable workhorse for identity and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Akkurat as Laurenz Brunner's first typeface, first released in April 2004 alongside the launch of a new Lineto website. They note its phenomenal success and influence in prompting a wider neo-modernist revival in type design. The family has grown from three original weights to five, with monospaced cuts and extensive non-Latin script support added in 2019.

name: LL Akkurat Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of the grotesque sans LL Akkurat, combining fixed-width spacing with the neutral, technically precise aesthetic of Swiss grotesque type.
summary: A monospaced offshoot of Laurenz Brunner's LL Akkurat, first released in 2005 and extended to four cuts in 2019.
description: Grown from a beta version that appeared shortly after the original LL Akkurat in 2004, the mono cut shares its parent's static construction and neutral, technically precise character. The family now comprises four cuts: Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic, all published together in 2019. Its restrained Swiss temperament makes it well suited to refined typesetting wherever a fixed-width face is needed.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Akkurat Mono as an extension of Laurenz Brunner's LL Akkurat, which aspired to embody technical precision, down-to-earth robustness, reliability and neutrality. A beta of the first monospaced cut appeared in spring 2004, used by Julia Born in her design of the Beauty and the Book publication, before the full release in 2005. A bold weight and italics were added in 2019, completing a small family of four cuts.

name: LL Atomgrad
designer: Stephan Müller, Yevgeniy Anfalov, Luca Pellegrini
classification_notes: A stencil typeface derived from Soviet-era industrial lettering, with forms built around the structural constraints and visual character of hand-cut stencils. Classified as display due to its highly specific, atmospheric origins and visual weight.
summary: A stencil typeface rooted in four Cyrillic letters photographed on a Chernobyl basement wall, expanded across Latin and Cyrillic by three designers over two decades.
description: Originally released as FF Chernobyl in 1998, Stephan Müller built the Latin alphabet from a minimal Cyrillic sample stencilled on a wall at the Chernobyl power plant, photographed by Igor Kostin in 1991. In 2020, Kyiv-born designer Yevgeniy Anfalov proposed a full Cyrillic counterpart, effectively returning the letters to their source, while redrawing the Latin in the process. Luca Pellegrini completed the project with a formal revision and extended character set, with Viktoriya Grabowska contributing refinements to the Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Atomgrad as originating from four Cyrillic letters — ЧАЭС — spotted by Stephan Müller in a newspaper photograph of the Chernobyl site taken in 1991. The stencil forms reflect Soviet-era industrial and commercial communication, where stencilled lettering was cheap and easily produced by hand. The family's expansion to include a full Cyrillic, led by Ukrainian designer Yevgeniy Anfalov and completed by Luca Pellegrini, is described as a dialectical homecoming for those four original letters.

name: LL Autoscape
classification_notes: A strictly monospaced, near-monolinear design tracing its lineage to bitmap renderings of Susan Kare's Monaco; its mechanical construction and equal character widths place it firmly in the monospace grotesque tradition.
summary: A monospace grotesque tracing its lineage through bitmap Monaco renderings, now redrawn from scratch by Minjong Kim with a variable weight range and a rounded variant.
description: Originally traced from the 9-point bitmap rendering of Susan Kare's Monaco in 1987 and first brought to print by Cornel Windlin for the 1999 Zurich exhibition Die Schweizer Autobahn, the design has been entirely redrawn by Kim with subtle optical corrections: a faint stroke contrast, squarish dots and improved letterforms that the original mechanical tracing had compromised. A variable weight axis running from Light to Bold, applied independently to both the standard and rounded versions, makes it viable for body text at small sizes as well as display work. The rounded variant retains the same strict monospace skeleton whilst substantially softening the cold mechanical register.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Autoscape as a readjustment and expansion of a monospace design that Cornel Windlin valued for its brutal simplicity and mechanical aesthetic, originally based on a 1987 tracing of Susan Kare's Monaco bitmap font. Minjong Kim redrawn all character shapes from scratch for a 2024 release, introducing subtle optical refinements and a variable weight range, and added a rounded version to broaden the family's tonal range whilst preserving its unmistakable monospace aesthetic.

name: LL Beans
classification_notes: Originally drawn with ruler and compass in the early 1970s and published by Letraset, Beans is a quirky geometric display typeface with stylistic alternates, built for expressive headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A digital reedition of Dieter Zembsch's 1973 Letraset competition winner, redrawn by Charles Grant and expanded by Sascha Bente, with original and new characters accessible via OpenType stylistic sets.
description: Originally drawn in secret at a German cosmetics company and submitted to Letraset's 1973 International Typeface Competition, where it was judged by Roger Excoffon, Herb Lubalin and Armin Hofmann among others. The typeface vanished from the Letraset catalogue by 1977, surfacing again only in the memory of obscure soul and reggae album sleeves. The 2020 edition by Sascha Bente completed the character set and reworked the design, while preserving Zembsch's original letterforms behind caps lock and routing new alternates through OpenType stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Beans as a quirky classic from the era of rub-down sheets that never lost its curious charm. First published by Letraset in 1973, the font disappeared from the catalogue by 1977 before Charles Grant rediscovered it and led a digital re-issue in 2008 with Zembsch's approval. A further overhaul by Sascha Bente in 2020 completed the character set and added new stylistic alternates, presenting the font as a virtual re-invention of the original Letraset design.

name: LL BIFF
classification_notes: An uppercase-only display face derived from New York throw-up graffiti letterforms, chunky and bold by design, placing it firmly in expressive display territory rather than any text category.
summary: An uppercase display face drawn from New York throw-up graffiti letterforms, originally created in 1995 and revised by Anatole Couteau in 2022 with a companion outline cut.
description: Originally drawn by Jonas Williamsson in 1995 as part of a commissioned identity project and published by Lineto in 1999, LL BIFF takes its forms directly from early New York graffiti throw-up lettering. The name derives from the Swedish adjective 'biffig', meaning muscle-bound or chunky, and the revised 2022 version sees Couteau redrawing the original outlines, completing the character set and adding an outline variant with graphic illustrations nodding to Michel Jarland's Mecanorma typeface Astral (1971). It remains uppercase only, suited to headlines, posters and identities that want weight and swagger.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL BIFF as first drawn in 1995 by Jonas Williamsson as single illustrations in Aldus FreeHand for a client project, then produced as a minimal uppercase headline font before becoming part of the visual language of Swedish-Swiss collective REALA. Originally published on Lineto's first website in 1999, the 2022 revision by Anatole Couteau redrew the original shapes, corrected outlines and added an Outline version with graphic illustrations referencing Mecanorma's Astral.

name: LL Blankenhorn
classification_notes: A family of three script fonts emulating German post-war hand-lettering, drawing on the calligraphic brush-painted style of Fritz Blankenhorn's book cover work. The three styles (Script, Formal, Typewriter) each represent a distinct thread of that lettering tradition.
summary: A three-style script family rooted in the post-war German hand-lettering of Fritz Blankenhorn, digitised by calligrapher Tobias-David Albert with over 5,000 contextual glyph combinations.
description: Blankenhorn references the distinctive brush-painted lettering of Fritz Blankenhorn (1921–2011), in-house designer at Deutscher Bücherbund, whose hand-lettering appeared on Max Frisch's Homo Faber in 1957 and became a signature style for the bestselling novels of Johannes Mario Simmel. Designer Tobias-David Albert identified three stylistic threads in Blankenhorn's book cover work and built a contextual system of more than 5,000 ligatures and alternate glyph combinations so that letters adjust their shape and position relative to their neighbours, producing text that reads as genuinely composed by hand. The family covers the full Latin character set with careful optimisation for 26 languages including those with complex diacritics.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Blankenhorn as a family of three script fonts emulating German lettering styles from the post-war era, closely referencing Fritz Blankenhorn's distinctive hand-lettering as seen on book covers for Homo Faber (1957) and Johannes Mario Simmel's novels. The project began in 2008 with traced versions by James Goggin but only advanced in 2014 when Tobias-David Albert, a calligrapher trained in type design, drew more than 5,000 unique glyph combinations to ensure each letter subtly adapts to its neighbours, giving the typeface the quality of text composed on the fly rather than assembled from fixed individual letters.

name: LL Bradford
classification_notes: Rooted in the Römische Antiqua tradition and its descendants Plantin and Times New Roman, Bradford is a text serif with hot-metal origins and transitional proportions, built for high-volume setting.
summary: A text serif developed over ten years by Laurenz Brunner, tracing a lineage from Römische Antiqua through Plantin and Times New Roman.
description: Conceived for high-volume text setting, Bradford draws its primary reference from the 1884 Römische Antiqua, the German design that fed directly into Pierpont's Plantin and Morison's Times New Roman. Fourteen cuts run from a delicate light to a powerful black, each with italics, and a monospaced companion extends the family for screen and code contexts. The design consciously preserves the physical presence of hot metal type while remaining comfortable across both print and digital applications.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Bradford as an all-purpose serif font family conceived as a versatile tool for high-volume text setting, developed and refined over ten years. Its primary historical reference is Römische Antiqua (1884), the popular German design that inspired Plantin (1913) and Times New Roman (1931). Brunner aimed to preserve the physical character of hot metal type across 14 cuts plus a monospace companion, resulting in a family that is functional yet playful across serious and informal contexts alike.

name: LL Bradford Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to a serif text family rooted in Römische Antiqua and the transitional serif tradition; it carries the typographic character of hot metal type into a fixed-pitch format.
summary: A single-cut monospaced companion to LL Bradford, carrying the physical character of hot metal type into a fixed-pitch serif format.
description: Drawn by Laurenz Brunner and released by Lineto in 2018, LL Bradford Mono is the fixed-pitch counterpart to the fourteen-cut LL Bradford family, itself developed over a decade with Römische Antiqua (1884) as its primary historical reference. That lineage runs through Plantin (1913) and Times New Roman (1931), and Brunner's aim throughout was to preserve the material presence of hot metal setting in a digital form. The mono cut offers a single Regular weight suited to text-heavy contexts where typewriter or terminal aesthetics are wanted alongside a grounded serif character.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Bradford Mono as the monospace companion to LL Bradford, an all-purpose serif family conceived for high-volume text setting and developed over ten years. The primary historical reference is Römische Antiqua (1884), a German design that influenced both Plantin and Times New Roman. Brunner's focus throughout was on preserving the physical presence and distinctive character of hot metal type, resulting in a family of fourteen cuts, with the mono as a single additional companion cut.

name: LL Brauer
designer: Pierre Miedinger, Philippe Desarzens, Marco Walser
classification_notes: Derived from a bespoke brewery branding typeface drawn by Pierre Miedinger, it functions as a display-weight sans with strong headline character and minimal text application.
summary: A revival of the bespoke branding typeface drawn by Pierre Miedinger for the Zürich Hürlimann brewery, resurrected from ink drawings after the brewery closed in 1997.
description: Originally drawn by Pierre Miedinger, nephew of Helvetica designer Max Miedinger, as the corporate face of Hürlimann brewery, the design appeared on everything from bottle labels to street posters before disappearing when Carlsberg acquired and shuttered the company. Elektrosmog revived it in 1999 for an arts festival on the former brewery site, later extended with additional weights by Philippe Desarzens and Marco Walser across two decades of incremental development. The full family of six weights, from Thin to Extra Bold, was released by Lineto in 2019, accompanied by a bonus set of the brewery's phased-out brand logos.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Brauer as referencing the former branding typeface of the Zürich-based Hürlimann brewery, drawn by Pierre Miedinger as part of its corporate design. After the brewery closed in 1997 and the typeface vanished with it, Elektrosmog revived it in 1999 with Miedinger's permission, extending the minimal original glyph set into a proper headline font. Further weights were added by Philippe Desarzens in 2006 and Marco Walser in 2011, before the complete six-weight family was released in 2019.

name: LL Brown
classification_notes: LL Brown is a geometric sans rooted in Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk, two early 20th-century geometric designs; its clean geometry, open apertures and systematic construction place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans by Aurèle Sack fusing Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk, grown into a large superfamily with Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic and Hebrew coverage.
description: Conceived at the suggestion of Zurich designers Lex Trüb and Urs Lehni in 2007, LL Brown draws on two historically loaded geometric sans serifs: Johnston Sans, the typeface of London's public transport, and Super Grotesk, which became ubiquitous in East Germany. Aurèle Sack refined and extended the family over more than a decade, adding Narrow and Condensed companions in 2022, a Mono subfamily, and decorative Graphic styles. Non-Latin scripts were developed in collaboration with specialists in Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic and Hebrew, making it one of the more thorough multi-script geometric sans families available.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Brown as a fusion of Edward Johnston's Johnston Sans (1916) and Arno Drescher's Super Grotesk (1930), two of the most influential geometric sans serifs, whose shared modernist roots span Cold War East and West. Designed by Aurèle Sack and first released in 2011, the family has since expanded with additional weights, Narrow and Condensed companions, Mono and Graphic subfamilies, and extensive non-Latin script support developed in collaboration with external specialists.

name: LL Brown Narrow
classification_notes: Rooted in the geometric sans tradition via Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk, LL Brown Narrow is a condensed-proportion companion to LL Brown with the same humanist-geometric character.
summary: A narrow-proportion companion to LL Brown, Aurèle Sack's geometric sans rooted in Edward Johnston's London Underground lettering and Arno Drescher's Super Grotesk.
description: Released in spring 2022 alongside the Condensed cut, LL Brown Narrow extends the LL Brown superfamily into tighter proportions while retaining the geometric-humanist character that has made the parent family a fixture of fashion and corporate identity work. Six weights from Thin to Black each carry matching italics, and the broader superfamily covers Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew and Arabic through collaborations with specialist script designers. The family has been adopted by clients including Celine, Moncler and Coach, reflecting its consistent performance across luxury and retail contexts.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Brown Narrow as a condensed companion to LL Brown, a geometric sans that fuses the influence of Edward Johnston's Johnston Sans (1916) and Arno Drescher's Super Grotesk (1930). Originally suggested by Zurich designers Lex Trüb and Urs Lehni in 2007 and first published in 2011, the family was expanded with Narrow and Condensed proportions in 2022. Lineto note that variable font files with weight, width and slant axes are currently in development.

name: LL Brown Condensed
classification_notes: Descended from Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk, two canonical geometric sans serifs; the Condensed sub-family maintains the geometric construction and humanist warmth of the parent LL Brown.
summary: A condensed companion to LL Brown, Aurèle Sack's geometric sans drawn from the shared heritage of Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk.
description: Published in spring 2022 after Sack revisited an earlier abandoned project, LL Brown Condensed offers six weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, in a tighter proportion alongside the matching Narrow cuts. The family sits within the broader LL Brown super-family, itself built on the formal principles of Edward Johnston's 1916 Underground lettering and Arno Drescher's 1930 Super Grotesk. Coverage extends to Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, making it practical for multi-script European work.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Brown Condensed as rooted in the groundbreaking work of Edward Johnston and Arno Drescher, whose Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk are among the most influential geometric sans serifs in history. The condensed and narrow families were conceived by Aurèle Sack in 2019 and published in spring 2022 as two full-width companions to the original LL Brown, with variable font files currently in development.

name: LL Brown Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of LL Brown, a geometric sans serif rooted in Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk; the mono cuts carry the same geometric construction into fixed-width spacing evoking daisywheel typewriter output.
summary: A fixed-width companion to LL Brown, extending Aurèle Sack's Johnston Sans and Super Grotesk synthesis into a form evoking IBM daisywheel typewriters.
description: Designed in 2020, LL Brown Mono carries the geometric character of LL Brown into two weights with matching italics, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek. Lineto describe the intent as creating monospaced fonts that are both beautiful and functional, as though LL Brown had an earlier life on an electric typewriter. The result is a rare mono cut with genuine typographic pedigree, well suited to code, editorial and interface contexts where warmth and geometric clarity matter.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Brown Mono as Aurèle Sack challenging himself to propose monospaced fonts both beautiful and functional for Latin, Greek and Cyrillic, carrying LL Brown's distinctive aesthetic to a new style as if it had had an earlier life on a daisywheel cartridge for an electric IBM typewriter.

name: LL Brown Graphic
classification_notes: Brown Graphic extends the geometric sans foundation of LL Brown into decorative display variants — Inline, Shaded and Cameo cuts — intended for branding and headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A set of decorative display cuts extending LL Brown into Inline, Shaded and Cameo variants, with a variable Cameo for screen use.
description: Designed by Aurèle Sack and released in 2019, Brown Graphic offers four Inline cuts, a Shaded cut and a Cameo cut with a full set of geometric shapes, all derived from the geometric sans foundation of LL Brown. The Cameo variant is also available as a variable font, inviting animated or parametric use on screen. The package draws on a tradition of decorative companion styles common to geometric typefaces of the 1930s, positioning itself for branding, editorial display and poster work.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Brown Graphic as a response to popular demand for more decorative renderings of LL Brown, offering additional panache for display and branding purposes in the tradition of geometric typefaces from the 1930s. The package contains four Inline cuts, a Shaded cut and a classic Cameo cut with a full set of geometric shapes; the variable Cameo version invites playful interaction on screen. Aurèle Sack plans to continue expanding the package.

name: LL Capital
classification_notes: A titling serif with extreme stroke contrast, diamond-shaped top serifs and an 18-degree italic slant, drawing directly from ITC Grouch and the Caslon reappropriations of 1970s New York. Its single weight and headline-first design place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight titling serif by Pablo Desportes that layers a new take on ITC Grouch and the Caslon reappropriations of 1970s New York onto a screen-capable display face.
description: Drawing on ITC Grouch, Benguiat Caslon and Caslon No.224, the design balances extreme stroke contrast and diamond-shaped top serifs against the legibility demands of digital screens. An 18-degree italic was devised from scratch using a systematic slanting approach, in the absence of italic precedents among the 1970s display Caslons. Originally sketched for a film title sequence in 2022, it was refined over three years before its release in 2025.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Capital as a graphic titling font in the spirit of 1970s Caslon reappropriations from the ITC circle, citing LSC Caslon, ITC Grouch, Benguiat Caslon and Caslon No.224 as precedents. Desportes reduced the extreme contrast of ITC Grouch to improve screen functionality, and devised a systematic 18-degree italic where no usable model existed. The face was first drawn for a 2022 film title sequence before being expanded into a full release published by Lineto in 2025.

name: LL Catalogue
classification_notes: Derived from Alexander Phemister's 1858 Old Style Antique No. 7 for Miller & Richard, with the robust upright verticals and near-clunky serifs characteristic of Victorian jobbing types that became the prototype for the Bookman genre.
summary: A meticulous digital redrawing of Alexander Phemister's 1858 Old Style Antique No. 7, redrawn by Nazareno Crea from new hot-metal prints of the original matrices.
description: First traced by M/M (Paris) from an old edition of Gulliver's Travels in 2002 and used for their French Vogue redesign in 2003, the typeface was subsequently redrawn from scratch after research into Phemister's original 1858 Miller & Richard design. Crea worked from prints taken off newly moulded hot-metal type, preserving the upright verticals, near-clunky serifs and particular imperfections of the Victorian original while making optical corrections in the transition to pixels. A 2025 overhaul expanded the character set, refined spacing and kerning, and introduced a variable font version.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Catalogue as a contemporary update of a 19th-century Scottish serif, originating from Phemister's 1858 design for Miller & Richard, Edinburgh. To capture the original, they had new hot-metal types moulded and used the resulting prints as the basis for a digital redrawing. The family includes Light and Bold weights alongside the original, plus small caps and an extensive glyph set, and is positioned as a jobbing text font suited to 8–16pt use. A 2025 update converted it to a variable font while preserving its quirky, authentic character.

name: LL Champion
classification_notes: A monolinear, angular slab serif with no curves, explicitly in the tradition of American varsity wood type and sports jersey lettering. The slab category fits given the prominent, block-like serifs; the geometric subcategory reflects its strictly mechanical, geometry-driven construction.
summary: A rigorously systematised take on American varsity slab lettering, designed by Weichi He with no curves and a duplexed fixed box width across three weights and two widths.
description: Weichi He applied a research-led, body-typeface methodology to the historically improvised tradition of US sports jersey lettering, producing a family of nine styles that strips the genre of every curve while retaining its blunt, block-like appeal. The duplexed structure keeps text reflow-free when switching weights, an unusual feature for display type that also reinforces the mechanical logic of the letterforms. A Carved variant adds a three-dimensional quality, and LL Champion Sans extends the family with a Shadow version suitable for layered colour work.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Champion as a contemporary take on angular slab serif typefaces long associated with US sports jerseys. Designer Weichi He applied extensive historical research, including turn-of-the-century wood types, and used extrapolation to determine an optimal range of three weights and two widths. The family introduces duplexing and a Carved variant as novelties to the genre, while LL Champion Sans extends it with a Shadow version for layered colour use.

name: LL Champion Cond
classification_notes: LL Champion is explicitly described as an angular slab serif in the varsity tradition, with strictly rectilinear construction and no curves throughout the glyph set. The condensed subfamily shares the same monolinear, mechanically constructed character as the full family.
summary: A rigorously systematised take on American varsity slab lettering, designed by Weichi He with no curves and a duplexed fixed box width across three weights.
description: Drawing from turn-of-the-century wood type and US sports jersey lettering, Weichi He applied an R&D process more typical of refined text typefaces to a genre usually defined by DIY improvisation. Every letterform is strictly rectilinear with no curves, and the three weights within each width are duplexed, sharing a fixed box width to prevent text reflow and reinforce the mechanical logic of the construction. The family extends to LL Champion Sans and layerable Carved and Shadow variants, with a tight set of historical alternates for additional character.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Champion as a contemporary take on the angular slab serif typefaces ubiquitous on US sports jerseys, designed by Weichi He through research into varsity history including turn-of-the-century wood types. Three weights and two widths were determined with extrapolation, the proportions fine-tuned before a strictly systematic approach produced a coherent glyph set with no curves. The family introduces duplexed spacing as a novelty to the genre, complemented by LL Champion Sans with Shadow variant, Carved versions for a 3-D effect, and carefully selected historical alternates.

name: LL Champion Sans
classification_notes: Champion Sans is the sans-serif counterpart to a slab-serif varsity family, featuring strictly rectilinear, monolinear letterforms without a single curve, owing more to mechanical geometry than optical correction.
summary: A sans-serif companion to LL Champion, built from strictly rectilinear, monolinear letterforms drawn from US varsity and wood-type traditions by Weichi He.
description: Where most varsity-style typefaces carry the charm of improvised lettering, Champion Sans applies a rigorous systematic process to the genre: no curves exist anywhere in the glyph set, proportions are fine-tuned rather than intuited, and the three weights within each width are duplexed so text does not reflow on weight change. The family pairs directly with the LL Champion slab serif, sharing proportions and weights across both styles. A Shadow version ships alongside the standard cuts and can be used independently or layered with a colour fill for dimensional effects.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Champion Sans as a companion to LL Champion, sharing the same weights and proportions as the slab serif. Designed by Weichi He using a strictly systematic approach that guarantees coherence across all versions, it includes a Shadow version usable independently or as a layered font with colour fill, making it a competitive design tool across professional applications.

name: LL Champion Sans Cond
classification_notes: A monolinear, angular sans with no curves, derived from the US varsity jersey tradition. Though it lacks serifs in this cut, its rigid geometric construction and display-oriented origins place it firmly in the display category with a geometric sans subcategory.
summary: The condensed sans cut of Weichi He's rigorously systematised take on the US varsity jersey letterform tradition, built without a single curve.
description: Part of the LL Champion family released in 2024, this condensed sans variant strips out the slab serifs while retaining the strictly angular, monolinear construction derived from turn-of-the-century American wood type and sports jersey lettering. The family is duplexed, meaning each weight shares a fixed box width to prevent text reflow and reinforce the mechanical character of the forms. A Shadow version allows independent use or layering with a colour fill, extending the display possibilities of the design.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Champion as a contemporary take on the angular slab serif typefaces long associated with US sports jerseys. Designer Weichi He subjected the genre to an R&D process more commonly applied to refined body typefaces, researching turn-of-the-century wood types before using extrapolation to determine an optimal range of three weights and two widths. The result contains no curves whatsoever, and introduces duplexing as a novelty to the genre. LL Champion Sans, sharing the same weights and proportions as the slab, adds a Shadow version suitable for layered colour use.

name: LL Circular
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a geometric grotesque in the tradition of Futura and Erbar Grotesk, with Brunner evolving the approach from purely geometric to one that incorporates warmth and measured idiosyncrasy by release.
summary: Laurenz Brunner's geometric grotesque, begun in 2008 and released in 2013, that tempers pure geometry with warmth to produce one of the most widely adopted sans-serifs of the past decade.
description: Developed over five years before its 2013 release, LL Circular sits in the lineage of Futura and Avenir but departs from strict geometry through carefully judged idiosyncrasies that make it more approachable at text sizes. Eight weights with matching italics cover editorial, advertising and identity work equally well. The family has since expanded to cover Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Arabic, Vietnamese and Devanagari, with additional script collaborators including Ilya Ruderman and Titus Nemeth.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Circular as a geometric sans-serif in eight weights that offers a fresh take on the pre-war German geometric grotesque tradition, citing Erbar Grotesk, Futura and Kabel as precedents. Begun in 2008, the design evolved from a purely geometric conception to one that balances functionality with warmth and idiosyncrasy, proving widely popular in editorial, advertising and branding. It is now available in Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew, Arabic, Vietnamese and Devanagari, including variable font versions.

name: LL Circular Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced cut of the geometric sans LL Circular, adapted to fixed-width spacing while retaining the circular construction and geometric character of the original family.
summary: A monospaced adaptation of Laurenz Brunner's geometric sans LL Circular, released in 2019 after a decade of testing to fit fixed-width applications without sacrificing the family's warmth.
description: Where the original LL Circular balanced geometric rigour with warmth, the mono cut preserves x-heights, weights and joints intact while conceding certain signature forms, such as the uppercase O, C and G, to the demands of fixed-width spacing. The result reads with a faintly mechanical quality that suits code editors and technical interfaces without abandoning the considered construction of Brunner's original 2013 release. A careful piece of type engineering that demonstrates how a typeface's character can survive a fundamental structural constraint.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Circular Mono as the product of a decade-long process of testing and refinement following the 2013 release of LL Circular. Adapting the circular letter shapes to fixed-width use posed its own challenges, but Brunner kept weights, x-heights and joints untouched, accepting changes only where the fixed-width constraint demanded them. The result extends Circular's range into the slightly mechanical territory of monospaced type.

name: LL Cobra
designer: Cornel Windlin, Samara Keller, Weichi He
classification_notes: LL Cobra is a display typeface built from geometric two-line letterforms derived from vernacular signage and low-brow type design. Its double-stroke construction and variable width modulation place it firmly in the display/decorative category with no conventional text application.
summary: A double-stroke geometric display typeface rooted in 1980s British sportswear signage, reborn as a variable font with adjustable stroke-width ratios.
description: Originally drawn by Cornel Windlin in 1996 from the logo of British high-street chain Cobra, itself a close relative of Ad Werner's 1972 Dubbeldik, the family has been rebuilt by Samara Keller as a variable font with a continuous range of black-to-white stroke ratios. At its extremes the modulation tips into Op-Art territory, making it as useful for pattern and surface design as for headline work. The full glyph set has been corrected and harmonised to meet contemporary standards while preserving the raw, mono-linear geometry of the original.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Cobra as a playful adaptation of vernacular type, tracing its origins to the distinctive white-on-green signage of the Cobra sportswear chain. The design drew on Ad Werner's cult classic Dubbeldik, and Cornel Windlin expanded its five original letterforms to a full alphabet for Lineto's first online catalogue in 1998. Samara Keller later revived the typeface as a variable font, correcting and harmonising the letterforms and introducing a modulated line-width axis that opens up possibilities in Op-Art and pattern-making.

name: LL Courier Sans
classification_notes: Derived from Courier by removing all serifs, then redrawn as a fully proportional sans-serif; its lineage is typewriter monospace but the released form is proportionally spaced.
summary: A proportional sans-serif grown from Courier by stripping away the serifs, originating as a student exercise at Ravensbourne in 1994 and refined into a full release in 2001.
description: James Goggin designed the first version during a 'Courier Only' typography exercise, circumventing the brief by removing every serif and correcting the spacing to proportional. The released family is now split into two branches: LL Courier Sans as a fully proportional version and LL Courier Sans Mono, drawn in 2019 to reconnect with the typewriter origins. The Light weight with italic gives it a deliberately understated, almost bureaucratic tone that suits academic and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Courier Sans as originating in 1994 when James Goggin, then a first-year student at Ravensbourne, responded to a 'Courier Only' exercise by cutting off all the serifs from the generic Macintosh system font. The first release in 2001 used carefully corrected proportional spacing rather than true monospacing. Over fifteen years later, while developing italic cuts, Lineto split the family into LL Courier Sans Mono (2019), restored to actual monospacing, and LL Courier Sans, a fully proportional version that inherited the original name.

name: LL Courier Sans Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans derived from Courier by removing all serifs, deliberately reconnected to its typewriter roots in the 2016 redraw with true fixed-width spacing.
summary: A sans-serif derived from Courier by removing every serif, first made as a student exercise in 1994 and later rebuilt as a true monospace in 2016.
description: James Goggin created the original as a deadpan riposte to a 'Courier only' brief at Ravensbourne in 1994, simply cutting the serifs from the system font. The first commercial release in 2001 used proportional spacing for visual harmony, but the 2016 redraw restored genuine fixed-width metrics, tying it back to its typewriter origins. Light and Regular weights are offered with italic cuts, and a separate proportional sibling, LL Courier Sans, carries the non-monospaced lineage forward.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Courier Sans Mono as originating in a 1994 student exercise by James Goggin, who removed the serifs from the generic Macintosh Courier to satisfy a 'Courier only' brief while technically breaking none of its rules. The first release in 2001 used corrected proportional spacing, but a 2016 redraw reinstated true monospacing to reconnect it with its typewriter heritage. Font engineering and mastering was handled by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: LL Cyan
classification_notes: Derived from Jan Tschichold's Redis-nib writing exercises, LL Cyan sits within the humanist sans tradition, drawing closer to Gill and Johnston than geometric models, with introduced contrast and rounded terminals reinforcing its warmth.
summary: A humanist sans drawn from Jan Tschichold's Redis-nib writing exercises, developed by Aurèle Sack into a 16-style family with three monospaced weights.
description: Rooted in a 1942 Tschichold school writing exercise that Aurèle Sack first encountered at ECAL in 2003, this family trades the geometric tendency common to Redis-nib alphabets for the softer curves of Gill and Johnston. Contrast was introduced to smooth joints and the terminals were carefully calibrated, preserving the warmth and rhythm of the handmade source. Each of the five proportional weights includes three sets of framed numerals and uppercase alphabets, and the three monospaced weights sit on the wider end of the genre with a raw, technical character.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Cyan as an art school writing exercise turned into a typeface proper, tracing its origins to François Rappo's 2003 ECAL sessions with Tschichold's Schriftkunde pages. Designed by Aurèle Sack and released in 2025, the family comprises 16 styles spanning five proportional weights and three monospaced weights, with font engineering and mastering by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: LL Cyan Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of a humanist sans derived from Jan Tschichold's Redis-nib writing exercises; monolinear construction with deliberate humanist warmth distinguishes it from geometric or neutral monospaceds.
summary: A monospaced humanist sans derived from Jan Tschichold's 1942 Redis-nib writing exercises, developed by Aurèle Sack into a five-weight family with an unusually warm and rhythmic character.
description: Rooted in a 2003 ECAL exercise referencing Tschichold's equal-stroke Redis-nib lettering, the design was fully developed by Sack from 2019 onwards. Where other Redis-inspired alphabets leaned geometric, Tschichold's originals stayed closer to the tradition of Gill and Johnston, and Sack amplified that tendency by introducing subtle contrast, smooth joints and calibrated rounded terminals. The three monospaced weights sit on the wider side of the genre, lending a raw, technical feel that sits alongside the warmth of the underlying hand-drawn source.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Cyan Mono as an art school writing exercise turned into a proper typeface, tracing its origins to François Rappo's introduction of ECAL students to Tschichold's 1942 Schriftkunde writing manual. Aurèle Sack preserved and extended the humanist qualities of Tschichold's Redis-nib sketches, modularising proportions while retaining warm curves and rounded terminals. The family totals 16 styles including five weights, each with three sets of framed numbers and upper-case alphabets, with the monospaced cuts sitting wider than the genre norm for a raw but friendly feel.

name: LL DMT VAR
classification_notes: The typeface sits firmly in the experimental/display category, rooted in Lineto's early 1990s interest in low-resolution printing technologies and the raw aesthetic of matrix printers and primitive thermo-printing devices, resulting in a distinctly unconventional letterform style.
summary: A variable typeface from Lineto rooted in the early 1990s desktop publishing era and the raw aesthetic of matrix printers and low-resolution display technologies.
description: Lineto's interest in the discarded technologies of the late 1980s and early 1990s forms the conceptual backbone here, drawing on the visual language of thermo-printing devices and matrix printers rather than the polished typefaces that came with the Apple OS. The VAR designation indicates a variable font format, extending the expressive range of a family already defined by its lo-fi origins. The specimen text, drawn from academic writing on psychedelic microdosing, reinforces the typeface's association with countercultural and fringe-scientific territory.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe the typeface as emerging from their early focus on letterforms shaped by the constraints of recently discarded technologies, including primitive thermo-printing devices, low-res screen displays and matrix printers. They situate this work within the post-1991 explosion of independent digital font production, made possible by tools like Fontographer and FontStudio and Adobe's opening of PostScript, which turned font creation into an unregulated and exploratory practice far removed from the standard Adobe and Linotype menus of the day.

name: LL Electa
classification_notes: A monospaced typewriter-style font derived from the Olivetti Pica typeface, featuring rationalized slab serifs and uniform character spacing characteristic of typewriter fonts.
summary: A rationalized revival of the Olivetti Pica typewriter font, drawn from the metal type bars rather than the softer impressions left on paper.
description: Luca Pellegrini worked directly from the metal type bars of a vintage Olivetti Studio 42 to arrive at sharper, more geometric outlines than the worn impressions typical of digital typewriter revivals. The design foregrounds features that paper could never reproduce cleanly, including perfectly rectangular slab serifs and precise curve dynamics, while the italics draw on separate historical typewriter models rather than the Olivetti source. Three weights, each with italic, stay close enough in tone to read as a typewriter family whilst exceeding anything achievable through manual variation of paper stock and keystroke pressure.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Electa as a thorough reappraisal of the Pica-style typewriter font used by Olivetti throughout the 20th century. Designer Luca Pellegrini, whose research into Xanti Schawinsky's 1930s work for Olivetti originally prompted the project, chose to model the letters from the metal type bars rather than their impressions on paper, yielding sharper outlines that represent an ideal form of the typeface. The resulting six-style family, developed between 2018 and 2026, avoids the nostalgic softness of many typewriter revivals and goes beyond a straightforward digitisation.

name: Electa Var
classification_notes: Electa Var is a variable typeface presented through animated, generative text demonstrations suggesting optical and structural variation; categorised as display given its experimental, visually led character.
summary: A variable typeface from Lineto whose page leans into generative and animated text demonstrations to show the range of its optical variation.
description: Electa Var is presented by Lineto through animated ASCII-style text compositions that visualise the typeface's variable behaviour across its axes. The page content foregrounds structural and rhythmic qualities rather than conventional weight or width labelling. Its experimental framing places it in display and editorial contexts where variable axis control is part of the visual concept.
foundry_blurb: Lineto present Electa Var through generative text animations and ASCII compositions that demonstrate the typeface's variable range, using repeating words such as 'radiation', 'structure' and 'energie' to reveal its optical and structural possibilities in motion.

name: Excellent
classification_notes: Based on a single-weight daisy wheel typewriter font originally drawn for Triumph-Adler in 1983, the design retains fixed-width, monospaced characteristics typical of typewriter typefaces, with stylistic references to Eurostile.
summary: A faithful digital revival of a 1983 Triumph-Adler daisy wheel typeface, drawn at Stempel by Lothar Kloth and Willy Deege and expanded into a working family by Stephan Müller.
description: Originally commissioned by Triumph-Adler from the Stempel type foundry, the design draws on Aldo Novarese's Eurostile as a formal reference while remaining a distinctly original typewriter face. Stephan Müller's 2008 rendering for Lineto adds a bold weight and italic cuts to the original single-weight design, giving the family practical range for contemporary use. Daisy wheel cartridges carrying the original Excellent font remain in production today, lending the revival an unusual continuity with its physical source.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe Excellent as a digital revival of a single-weight typewriter font originally commissioned by Triumph-Adler from the Stempel type foundry in 1983. Drawn by Lothar Kloth and Willy Deege with stylistic references to Eurostile, it was released on Lineto in 2008 by Stephan Müller, who added bold and italic cuts to the original design. Font engineering and mastering was handled by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: LL Geigy
classification_notes: A sans-serif with pronounced thick-thin contrast and horizontal terminals, drawing from pre-Helvetica Swiss hand-lettering and custom typography; its eccentric curves and tightly closed forms sit closer to humanist than geometric or grotesque traditions.
summary: A sans-serif drawn from pre-Helvetica Swiss lettering, built around pronounced thick-thin contrast and eccentric curves that counter the austerity of the era's standard grotesques.
description: Robert Huber spent over three years developing LL Geigy, taking as his starting point Karl Gerstner's 1953 masthead lettering for Geigy Berater and the broader tradition of whimsical, hand-drawn Swiss typography that Helvetica's dominance later obscured. The family runs to four weights with italics, plus two monospaced weights in which the contrast creates unusual spatial tension, widening and narrowing letterforms in ways that amplify rather than suppress their idiosyncratic character. Compact metrics and tightly closed apertures with horizontal terminals make it legible at small sizes despite its expressive proportions.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Geigy as inspired by Swiss graphic design from the decade before Helvetica and Univers standardised the aesthetic landscape, specifically citing Karl Gerstner's 1953 masthead for the Geigy Berater periodical. Robert Huber's design takes the playfully dynamic, extravagant curves found across Basel poster, signage and printed matter of the 1950s and develops them into a four-weight family with italics and two monospaced weights, paying homage to a free-spirited strain of mid-century Swiss typography that has received little recognition.

name: LL Geigy Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans-serif with pronounced thick-thin contrast, unusual for the mono genre; derived from LL Geigy, a display-leaning eccentric sans rooted in pre-Helvetica Swiss graphic design.
summary: Two monospaced weights derived from LL Geigy, carrying the family's eccentric thick-thin contrast into fixed-width territory inspired by pre-Helvetica Swiss graphic design.
description: Designed by Robert Huber and released by Lineto in October 2023, the mono variant extends LL Geigy's playfully extravagant curves and pronounced thick-thin contrast into a fixed-width format — territory where such contrast is almost unheard of. The forced equal width amplifies certain quirks: rounded uppercase forms become more conspicuous when narrowed, while some lowercase letters grow curvier through widening. The result sits at the intersection of mid-century Swiss eccentricity and functional monospaced type, suited to editorial, brand and typographic contexts where a standard code face would be too neutral.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Geigy Mono as a complement to the LL Geigy family, leading designer Robert Huber into largely uncharted territory. Monospaced sans-serifs with pronounced contrast are unusual, and the fixed-width constraint makes rounded uppercase shapes more conspicuous while widening certain lowercase letters. Huber is said to have fused an experimental approach with sustained functionality, paying homage to a free-spirited aspect of mid-century Swiss typography that has found little recognition. A variable, duplexed version of LL Geigy is in development and available on request.

name: LL Geigy Duplex
classification_notes: A uniwidth variable grotesque derived from LL Geigy, sharing its industrial Swiss heritage and sans-serif structure across a full weight range with matching italics.
summary: A uniwidth variable grotesque derived from LL Geigy, engineered so that every glyph occupies identical space across all weights and styles, eliminating text reflow when switching between them.
description: Designed by Robert Huber, LL Geigy Duplex applies the duplex model — a principle from hot-metal typesetting — to a contemporary variable font spanning Light to Black with matching italics. Because spacing and kerning values are fixed across the entire weight and style range, designers can switch weights or animate between them without any layout shift. This makes it particularly suited to screen interfaces where buttons, labels or running text need to transition between weights without redrawing surrounding content.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Geigy Duplex as an exercise in Optical Design Engineering. Designer Robert Huber adapted the full glyph widths, proportions, metrics and kerning from the original Geigy fonts into a variable font built on the duplex model, where every glyph occupies the same space from Light to Black and Roman to Italic. The result performs in both static and animated contexts, removing the layout shifts that occur when switching weights in screen-based interfaces.

name: LL Geigy Duplex Var
classification_notes: Described by Lineto as an eccentric sans serif; the duplex variable format and geometric-yet-quirky character set it apart from conventional grotesque or humanist classifications.
summary: A uniwidth variable font by Robert Huber that keeps glyph spacing fixed across its full weight and slant range, preventing text reflow when axes change.
description: Based on LL Geigy, this variable font applies the duplex model — a concept from hot-metal typesetting — to a contemporary variable format, ensuring glyphs occupy identical space from Light to Black and from Roman to Italic. That fixed-width behaviour makes it particularly suited to screen interfaces where weight or style transitions occur on hover, and to animated type where gradual axis changes can produce optical effects without layout shifts. Font engineering and mastering was handled by Alphabet, Berlin.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Geigy Duplex Var as an exercise in Optical Design Engineering, developed by Robert Huber as a variable font built on the duplex model, where every glyph maintains identical spacing and kerning across the entire weight and slant range. They trace the uniwidth concept to early 20th-century hot-metal typesetting, noting its renewed relevance for screen interfaces and animated type applications where text reflow would otherwise occur.

name: Geigy Cond Duplex
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque rooted in the visual language of mid-century Swiss industrial and pharmaceutical typographic culture, with the notable technical property of duplex (uniwidth) spacing across all weights.
summary: A condensed, duplex grotesque by Robert Huber, developed as part of the LL Geigy family, where every weight shares identical character widths to allow weight switching without reflow.
description: Designed by Robert Huber as a technical offshoot of LL Geigy, the Cond Duplex variant applies uniwidth spacing across its five weights from Light to Black, so that switching weight never disturbs the layout. The family draws on the graphic language of mid-century Swiss chemical and pharmaceutical industry print, particularly the Geigy company's distinctive typographic output. Italic companions accompany each weight, and the duplex engineering makes it well-suited to variable font applications where text reflow would otherwise be a problem.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe Geigy Cond Duplex as an exercise in Optical Design Engineering. For the variable version of LL Geigy, designer Robert Huber worked with duplexed fonts, also known as uniwidth: proportional typefaces whose spacing remains consistent across weights.

name: Geigy Cond Duplex Var
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as an eccentric sans serif, the condensed duplex variable is a uniwidth typeface with fixed spacing and kerning across its full weight and slant range, placing it firmly in the sans-serif category with an idiosyncratic, display-leaning character.
summary: A condensed variable font by Robert Huber built on a duplex model, so glyph widths and spacing remain fixed across the full weight and slant range without text reflow.
description: Derived from LL Geigy, this variable font applies a uniwidth structure dating back to hot-metal typesetting, where consistent glyph widths allowed compositors to switch weights or styles without resetting entire paragraphs. All metrics and kerning were re-analysed across the complete weight range to preserve this fixed-width behaviour while supporting a continuous weight axis from Light to Black and a slant axis from Roman to Italic. The format is particularly suited to screen interfaces and animated type, where transitions between weights or styles can be rendered gradually without layout shifts.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Geigy Cond Duplex Var as a variable font rooted in the duplex or uniwidth model, where each glyph occupies the same horizontal space regardless of weight or style. Designed by Robert Huber and engineered by Alphabet Berlin, the family was developed through exhaustive study of glyph widths, proportions and kerning across the full weight range of the original Geigy fonts. Lineto note its particular suitability for animated applications and interactive interfaces where switching between weights or axes would otherwise cause text reflow.

name: Gravur
classification_notes: Gravur is a Swiss-influenced grotesque sans-serif with condensed cuts and thin weights, sitting within the neutral European grotesque tradition suited to corporate and pharmaceutical contexts.
summary: A Swiss grotesque from Lineto with a prominent condensed family, shown extensively in pharmaceutical and corporate typesetting contexts.
description: The family includes condensed and italic cuts, with specimen copy drawn from pharmaceutical corporations such as Bayer, Novartis and Pfizer, suggesting its intended territory of corporate and scientific communication. The condensed variant is shown at both display and text sizes, carrying technical product names and dense body copy with equal composure. A workmanlike grotesque that earns its keep in information-heavy, institutional settings rather than expressive or editorial ones.
foundry_blurb: Lineto present Gravur through specimen settings featuring pharmaceutical and life sciences copy at a range of sizes, demonstrating the family across condensed and italic variants in both display and small text applications.

name: LL Grey
classification_notes: Lineto describe it as a sans serif born of a French grotesque, with the stroke endings characteristic of that genre; it draws formal cues from 19th century grotesque proportions, placing it squarely within that subcategory.
summary: A French grotesque revival by Aurèle Sack that widens the idiosyncratic proportions of 19th century grotesques into a screen-friendly text face.
description: Originating as Sack's ECAL diploma project in 2004 under the name AS Gold, LL Grey was refined and extended for release by Lineto in 2018. It carries the characteristic rude stroke endings of the French grotesque genre while achieving a contemporary readability suited to on-screen use. The family has since grown to include Greek, Cyrillic and a monospaced variant, with script extensions produced in collaboration with Viktoriya Grabowska and Panos Haratzopoulos.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Grey as a sans serif born of a French grotesque, retaining the cabaret-like stroke endings of the genre. Originally titled AS Gold and debuted as Aurèle Sack's ECAL diploma project in 2004, it has since been distilled and extended into a playful yet highly readable text font with a contemporary, screen-friendly appearance. Sack applied his characteristic precision to widen the idiosyncratic proportions of 19th century grotesques, continuing his approach with LL Purple and LL Brown of drawing on historical predecessors while reworking them into something current.

name: LL Grey Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of LL Grey, itself a French grotesque sans-serif. The mono designation and specimen context confirm a fixed-width cut derived from grotesque formal conventions.
summary: A monospaced offshoot of LL Grey, carrying the rude stroke endings and idiosyncratic proportions of 19th-century French grotesques into a fixed-width format.
description: Derived from Aurèle Sack's LL Grey, itself born as a diploma project at ECAL in 2004, this mono cut retains the cabaret-like stroke endings that define the French grotesque genre. The fixed-width format makes it well suited to coding environments and screen-based text work where personality is still wanted. It sits within a colour-named trilogy alongside LL Purple and LL Brown.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Grey as a sans-serif born of a French grotesque, with rude, cabaret-like stroke endings characteristic of the genre. Originally called AS Gold and debuted as part of Aurèle Sack's 2004 ECAL diploma project, it was later extended into a playful yet highly readable text font with a contemporary, screen-friendly appearance, widening the idiosyncratic proportions of 19th-century grotesques with Sack's signature surgical precision.

name: LL Heymland
classification_notes: A titling typeface derived from Roman capitals, drawing on Koch Antiqua's decorative early-1920s character while being systematically geometricised; suited only to display use in its single-weight, all-caps orientation.
summary: A titling typeface by Yevgeniy Anfalov derived from a calligraphic study attributed to Soviet graphic artist Solomon Telingater, itself a geometricised reading of Rudolf Koch's Koch Antiqua.
description: Anfalov traced the letterforms from an unsigned, undated single-page study found in Telingater's estate, stripping what he considered superfluous detail and unifying the shapes before reintroducing optical corrections to avoid a purely mechanical result. The typeface covers full Latin and Cyrillic character sets, with Cyrillic shapes drawn partly from a 1970 Russian manual on type design, making the whole family a chain of interpretation running from 1920s Germany through Soviet-era Moscow to the present. Custom ligatures drawn for its debut in TATE ETC. magazine in summer 2020 have since been added to the release font.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Heymland as an expansion of an archive discovery attributed to Telingater, a founding member of the constructivist October Group alongside Rodchenko, Lissitzky and Eisenstein. The undated study refers to Koch Antiqua and likely dates from the early 1960s. Anfalov took a distinctly digital approach, unifying and simplifying the source drawings before designing a large number of new glyphs to produce a typeface suited to contemporary projects, covering Latin and Cyrillic from the outset — a first for Lineto.

name: LL Hoboken High
classification_notes: The family includes both a sans and a slab serif cut, both with a geometric, constructed character typical of display-oriented sports and institutional lettering. The sans is the primary classification given its dominant use in the page specimens.
summary: A locker-room display typeface in sans and slab serif cuts, originally released in 1998 and designed by Nico Schweizer out of Hoboken, NJ.
description: Designed while Schweizer was based in Hoboken, the typeface carries the constructed, blocky character of sports jerseys and institutional signage. Available in both sans and slab serif variants since its 1998 release, it is currently being expanded to four cuts. The family suits display work where a bold, athletic register is required.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe Hoboken High as their locker room classic, available in Slab Serif and Sans since 1998, and currently undergoing a thorough update with an extension to four cuts.

name: LL Home
classification_notes: Descended from Futura via Linotype's Spartan and Spartan Classified, LL Home carries the geometric sans lineage but with idiosyncratic, historically accumulated letterform quirks that distinguish it from conventional geometric revival work.
summary: A geometric sans with a century of technological distortion baked into its letterforms, tracing a lineage from Futura through Linotype's Spartan Classified to a fully functional contemporary family.
description: Clemens Piontek spent several years maturing the shapes of LL Home, working from Linotype's 1951 Spartan Classified, a hot-metal newspaper font whose letters were individually compressed, shortened and altered purely for legibility at 5.5 pt. Those functional distortions, carried forward through phototypesetting and a hasty PostScript conversion, became the typeface's character. The family now runs to four weights with italics, a variable axis and two rounded subfamilies, and performs unusually well across both body text and display settings.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Home as capturing an astonishing century-long journey of an alphabet shaped and reshaped by technology, time and taste. Starting as a US version of Futura, the design passed through Linotype's Spartan, a classified newspaper cut, phototypesetting and PostScript before being revived for the identity of Skulptur Münster 2017 by Lehni and Trüb, then expanded by Piontek into a full family over the following years. The incoherent details from its newspaper origins are presented not as flaws but as the source of its unmistakable charm and surprising functional range.

name: LL Home
classification_notes: Rooted in Futura's lineage via the Spartan Classified hot-metal variant, LL Home is a grotesque sans with eccentric proportions and terminals that defy pure geometric classification.
summary: A revival of Spartan Classified — Linotype's distorted 1951 hot-metal derivative of Futura — redeveloped by Clemens Piontek into an eight-style family with an eccentric but highly functional charm.
description: Originally pressed into service for the Skulptur Münster 2017 identity by graphic designers Lex Trüb and Urs Lehni, LL Home draws on Spartan Classified's paradoxical proportions: letters individually condensed, shortened or given arbitrary terminals to survive 5.5pt newspaper printing, which at larger scales become oddly appealing design features. Piontek redrew the shapes over several years, adding three weights plus italics to the original book cut, and the family is also available as a variable font. All-caps settings exploit the wide, stern uppercase while lowercase body text behaves with an entirely different rhythm, making the two registers feel almost like separate typefaces that nonetheless cohere.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Home as capturing a century-long journey shaped by technology, time and taste, tracing a lineage from US Futura through Linotype's Spartan to the single-size Spartan Classified of 1951, whose oddities Piontek has matured over several years into a version that is, in their words, extremely handy in use for both body text and headlines, in print and on screen.

name: LL Ivory
classification_notes: LL Ivory derives from 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, reinterpreted with reduced contrast, pronounced serifs and strict geometric construction. The family spans display, text and mono subfamilies, with the display cuts retaining the extended proportions and expressive character of the historical sources.
summary: A geometric-grid serif by Aurèle Sack, rooted in 19th-century American wood type and refined across text, display and mono subfamilies for editorial and identity use.
description: Developed initially as a custom titling face for Vogue Hommes Paris in 2016, LL Ivory extended into a full family through an unusual reverse process: the text proportions were derived first, then the display cuts opened outward. Sack's strict grid methodology keeps the letterforms surgically precise despite their expressive, extended proportions, with contrast reduced from the historical sources to give the text cuts grip at small sizes. The three monospaced cuts add a functional dimension unusual in a serif family of this character.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ivory as Aurèle Sack's first full serif family, begun in 2016 when Sack was appointed typographic consultant to Vogue Hommes Paris and commissioned to design an expressive yet elegant serif to accompany his own LL Brown. Drawing on 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, Sack retained their extended proportions while reducing contrast and refining the serifs, guided throughout by his characteristic method of strict grids and geometric construction. After years of testing in art catalogues, books, magazines and identities, the family is now available for wide release.

name: LL Ivory
classification_notes: Derived from 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, LL Ivory is a high-contrast serif with pronounced shapes and geometric construction, available in both display and text variants with reduced contrast, and monospaced cuts.
summary: A serif family by Aurèle Sack rooted in 19th-century American wood type, refined through geometric construction and extended into display, text and monospaced cuts.
description: Initiated in 2016 as a commission for Vogue Hommes Paris, LL Ivory developed from a titling serif inspired by American wood type and poster fonts into a full family spanning display and text grades. Sack reduced contrast and trimmed the serifs for the text version, producing a sturdy, clear-cut body type that retains some extravagance from the display styles. Three monospaced cuts extend the family further, and the whole range carries the surgically precise geometry characteristic of Sack's work at NORM.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ivory as Aurèle Sack's first in-depth full serif family, born from a Vogue Hommes Paris commission for an expressive yet elegant titling font. Sack drew on 19th-century American wood type, keeping extended proportions while refining contrast and adding capricious details, then worked in a reverse process to derive text styles of narrower proportions. The family's strict geometric construction, reduced contrast for text use, and three monospaced cuts give it a clean, sturdy character tested across art catalogues, books, magazines and graphic identities before its 2023 wide release.

name: LL Ivory Display
classification_notes: Derived from 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, LL Ivory Display retains extended proportions with strong contrast and pronounced serifs, placing it firmly in the high-contrast display serif tradition with a geometric underpinning.
summary: A high-contrast display serif by Aurèle Sack rooted in 19th-century American wood type, refined through strict geometric grids for fashion editorial and identity use.
description: Originating as a commission for Vogue Hommes Paris, LL Ivory Display keeps the extended proportions of its wood-type sources while reducing contrast and trimming the serifs to a surgically clean finish. Sack's grid-based construction method, inherited from his time at NORM, gives the letterforms an unusual precision for a typeface with such expressive origins. The Display subfamily sits alongside the text-weight LL Ivory and three monospaced cuts, providing a cohesive system across large titling and smaller setting.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ivory as Aurèle Sack's first full serif family, initiated in 2016 from a Vogue Hommes Paris commission calling for an expressive yet elegant titling serif to accompany LL Brown. Sack drew on 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, refining their proportions and contrast through his characteristic geometric grid method. After years of testing in art catalogues, books, magazines and brand identities, the complete family including Display, text and Mono subfamilies was released in 2023.

name: LL Ivory Display Var
classification_notes: A high-contrast display serif with extended proportions derived from 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, refined through geometric construction into a distinct contemporary formulation.
summary: A variable display serif by Aurèle Sack, drawing on 19th-century American wood type and refined through strict geometric construction for editorial and titling use.
description: Originally commissioned by Vogue Hommes Paris as a titling face to accompany LL Brown, the display version retains the very extended proportions of its American wood type sources while reducing contrast and sharpening the serifs into something more precise. Construction follows Sack's characteristic grid method, giving even the expressive display cuts a surgically clean geometry. The broader LL Ivory family extends to text and monospaced variants, tested across art catalogues, books and magazine work before wide release.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ivory as Aurèle Sack's first full serif family, initiated in 2016 from a commission for Vogue Hommes Paris and developed over years of refinement. Inspired by 19th-century American wood type, Sack applied his method of reassessing and reinterpreting historic sources, keeping extended proportions while reducing contrast and refining the serifs. The family includes display, text and three monospaced cuts, all built on the strict geometric grids characteristic of his practice.

name: LL Ivory Mono
classification_notes: LL Ivory Mono is the monospaced subfamily of a high-contrast serif family, making it a rare serif monospaced design with pronounced serifs and reduced contrast suited to text use.
summary: The monospaced subfamily of Aurèle Sack's LL Ivory, bringing the family's sturdy serif construction and surgically clean geometry into fixed-width cuts.
description: Drawn by Aurèle Sack, LL Ivory Mono takes the reduced-contrast serif shapes of the LL Ivory text family and applies them to a monospaced grid — an unusual combination that gives the cuts both typographic character and technical utility. Three weights from Light to Medium each come with italics, making it usable across a range of settings where a serif mono would ordinarily be a compromise. The family originated in a 2016 commission for Vogue Hommes Paris and was developed over several years through editorial, book and identity work before wide release.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ivory Mono as one of three subfamilies within the LL Ivory system, alongside the text and display families. The full family emerged from Aurèle Sack's commission for a titling serif for Vogue Hommes Paris, drawing on 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, reworked through his method of strict grids and geometric construction. The monospaced cuts extend the family's range and are noted for their surgically clean and precise feel.

name: LL Ivory Mono Var
classification_notes: Rooted in 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, LL Ivory is a high-contrast serif with pronounced shapes; the Mono Var cut extends the family into monospaced territory while retaining the same geometric rigour and sturdy character.
summary: A monospaced variable cut of Aurèle Sack's serif family, originally commissioned for Vogue Hommes Paris and rooted in 19th-century American wood type.
description: LL Ivory began as a titling commission for Vogue Hommes Paris in 2016, drawing on American wood type and poster fonts before Sack's strict grid-based method pushed it into its own distinct territory. The Mono Var subset brings three monospaced cuts into a variable format, pairing the family's sturdy, low-contrast serifs and geometric construction with the fixed-width spacing more common to code or editorial contexts. Developed over several years and tested across art catalogues, magazines and identities, the family has the surgically precise quality characteristic of Sack's work at NORM and beyond.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ivory as Aurèle Sack's first in-depth development of a full serif family, initiated in 2016 when Sack was appointed typographic consultant to Vogue Hommes Paris. The commission called for an expressive yet elegant serif to accompany LL Brown; Sack found his source material in 19th-century American wood type and poster fonts, then applied his signature method of reassessment and geometric modulation to arrive at something distinctly his own. The family was tested across art catalogues, books, magazine work and graphic identities before its wide release in 2023.

name: LL Jumping Jack
designer: Dieter Zembsch, Charles Grant, Sascha Bente
classification_notes: A geometric, modular display face built from overlapping jointed components resembling a jumping jack toy; purely display in intent with no aspiration toward body text use.
summary: A modular display face originally designed for Mecanorma dry-transfer sheets in 1975, built from overlapping jointed components that mimic the articulation of a toy jumping jack.
description: Originally drawn by Dieter Zembsch and first published by Mecanorma in 1975, it won him Letraset's 1973 type design competition in an earlier form. Each letterform is constructed from overlapping segments with small circular holes representing joints, giving the face a mechanical, toy-like character that sits somewhere between abstract and legible. The digital version was first sketched by Charles Grant in 2011, then fully expanded and completed by Sascha Bente at Lineto in 2019, with the final release in 2020.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Jumping Jack as the follow-up to LL Beans, both originally published as dry-transfer decals by Dieter Zembsch in the early 1970s. The font's components overlap like parts of an actual jumping jack, with small round holes standing in for joints. Charles Grant began the digital adaptation in 2011, and Sascha Bente overhauled and completed it in 2019, with Zembsch's approval, before Lineto released it in 2020.

name: LL Kleisch
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a serif typeface; the design draws on transitional features shared between Latin serif and Ming (old-style CJK) typefaces, with rationalised stroke models and contrast informed by both traditions.
summary: A serif designed by Chiachi Chao to sit harmoniously alongside Ming CJK typefaces, derived from shared transitional features in Latin and Chinese printing traditions.
description: Chiachi Chao identified formal correspondences between Latin serif and Ming letterforms that emerged during their respective periods of rationalisation, using those shared properties of stroke, ductus and proportion as the foundation for the design. The result works both as a standalone Latin text typeface and as a companion to a wide range of Ming fonts, addressing the persistent quality problem of Latin letterforms embedded within CJK type systems. Three optical subfamilies cover text, headline and display settings, supplemented by a variable family spanning upright and italic axes.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Kleisch as a serif typeface designed specifically to complement old-style CJK typefaces, addressing the stylistic incompatibility that designers in Asian countries routinely face when mixing English with Ming script. Designer Chiachi Chao identified formal features common to both Latin serif and Ming shapes that converged during their respective periods of rationalisation, using these shared qualities of writing speed, ductus and stroke models to build a typeface that excels both in combination with Ming and as a standalone Latin type.

name: LL Kleisch Headline
classification_notes: Based on Baroque and Neoclassical models including Van Djik, Kis and Fleischman, with elevated contrast optimised for large sizes; the design bridges formal features of Latin serif and CJK Ming typefaces.
summary: A Baroque-derived Latin serif by Chiachi Chao, built from the ground up to sit harmoniously alongside CJK Ming typefaces in bilingual typesetting.
description: Designed by Chiachi Chao and released in 2024, the family draws on Van Djik, Kis and Fleischman to identify formal features in Latin serif letterforms that correspond to characteristics found in Ming characters.
