The Headline subfamily increases contrast relative to the text-size LL Kleisch, making it suited to large-scale settings. A variable version with weight and contrast axes allows fine-grained matching to specific Ming designs.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Kleisch as addressing the daily challenge faced by designers in Asian countries where English text must coexist with CJK type, and where available Latin letterforms in Ming fonts are often inadequate. Chiachi Chao approached this by identifying Baroque and Neoclassical Latin features that converge with Ming characteristics in ductus, stroke model and proportion. The Headline family adds increased contrast for large sizes, and a fully variable version with weight and contrast axes enables precise matching to a wide range of Ming typefaces in common use.

name: LL Kleisch Display
classification_notes: Kleisch is grounded in Baroque and Neoclassical Latin serif models, specifically Van Dijk, Kis and Fleischman, giving it the transitional serif character of those historical sources while being purpose-built for pairing with CJK Ming typefaces.
summary: A Latin serif designed by Chiachi Chao to pair with CJK Ming typefaces, drawing on Baroque and Neoclassical models to align with shared formal features across scripts.
description: Designed from the observation that Latin serif and Ming typefaces converged in stroke models and ductus during their respective periods of rationalisation, Kleisch synthesises features from Van Dijk, Kis and Fleischman to produce a Latin that sits comfortably alongside a wide range of Ming designs. Three static optical families cover text, headline and display sizes, each with five weights and italics, and a fully variable version adds axes for weight and contrast for finer matching to specific CJK contexts. The typeface won a Swiss Design Award in 2023 before its 2024 release.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Kleisch as a serif typeface designed specifically to complement old-style CJK typefaces, addressing the everyday incompatibility between Latin serifs and Ming scripts in terms of contrast, proportion and stroke model. Designer Chiachi Chao identified formal features in Baroque and Neoclassical Latin types that align surprisingly closely with features found in contemporary Ming characters, basing Kleisch on Van Dijk, Kis and Fleischman as a result. The family comes in three static optical styles, each with five weights and italics, plus a variable version with weight and contrast axes.

name: LL Kleisch Variable
classification_notes: Kleisch is grounded in Baroque and Neoclassical models, drawing on Van Dijk, Kis and Fleischman, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition with deliberate formal alignment to Ming typeface conventions.
summary: A transitional serif by Chiachi Chao designed from the ground up to pair with Ming CJK typefaces, drawing on Baroque and Neoclassical models to bridge formal features across two writing systems.
description: Chiachi Chao identified shared structural characteristics between Latin transitional serifs and Ming typefaces — similar ductus, stroke models and proportional logic born of parallel rationalisation processes — and built Kleisch around those convergences. The family spans three optical grades (text, headline and display) each with six weights and italics, plus a variable version with weight and contrast axes for fine-tuning against specific Ming designs. The result functions as a purpose-built Latin companion to CJK type as well as a capable standalone text face. Kleisch won a Swiss Design Award in 2023 before its full release in 2024.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Kleisch as a serif typeface designed specifically to complement old-style CJK typefaces (Ming), tackling the persistent challenge of stylistic incompatibility when Latin and CJK scripts are set together. Designer Chiachi Chao identified formal features common to both traditions during their respective periods of rationalisation and synthesised them into a typeface that aligns naturally with Ming conventions. The family includes regular, headline and display grades, each with six weights plus italics, alongside a fully variable version with weight and contrast axes to match a wide range of Ming typefaces in common use.

name: LL Kristall
classification_notes: Brunner explicitly positions LL Kristall as a humanist sans, completing a trilogy alongside the neo-grotesque LL Akkurat and geometric LL Circular. Its low contrast, vertically cut terminals and calligraphic influence from Johnston and Calvert confirm the humanist classification.
summary: The third entry in Laurenz Brunner's sans serif trilogy for Lineto, drawing on Johnston and Calvert to produce a humanist sans with low contrast and vertically cut terminals.
description: Completing a sequence that began with the neo-grotesque LL Akkurat in 2004 and the geometric LL Circular in 2013, LL Kristall takes a humanist direction shaped by Brunner's time in London and the typographic legacies of Edward Johnston's Underground type and Margaret Calvert's road signage. Vertically cut terminals and low contrast distinguish it from softer humanist contemporaries, lending it more structural firmness than is typical of the genre. The weight range runs from Light to Ultra Black, making it workable across minimal editorial settings and larger display applications alike.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Kristall as the third in a series of sans serifs by Laurenz Brunner, following the neo-grotesque LL Akkurat and geometric LL Circular. Brunner's brief time at St. Martin's School of Art in London left a lasting imprint, with Johnston's Tube lettering and Calvert's road signage informing the design. The typeface balances minimalism and calligraphy through low contrast and vertically cut terminals, with a weight range from Light to Ultra Black intended to carry it from subtle, restrained work through to bold, commanding applications.

name: LL Le Corbusier
classification_notes: Derived from antique zinc lettering stencils common in European hardware and art supply shops throughout the 20th century, the family belongs firmly in the display category as a stencil type. The rugged, interrupted letterforms are characteristic of the stencil display tradition.
summary: A family of four stencil fonts redrawn from antique zinc lettering stencils, associated with the architectural drawings of Le Corbusier and widely used across 20th-century Europe.
description: Originally digitised in the 1990s and early 2000s, the family was fully redrawn by Chiachi Chao and re-released in spring 2024 across three styles: a standard stencil, the Oldface cut associated with Le Corbusier's own architectural plans, and a Cameo version based on the physical silhouette of the original zinc stencils. Each style is manually kerned with an expanded glyph set, balancing refinement against the rugged irregularity of the source material. The result sits well in editorial, architectural and typographic work that calls for a stencil with genuine historical grounding.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Le Corbusier as relating to three sets of antique zinc lettering stencils of the kind sold in European hardware and art supply shops throughout the 20th century. The current versions, redrawn from scratch by Chiachi Chao and released in 2024, replace earlier digital interpretations by Nico Schweizer (1999) and Philippe Desarzens for Elektrosmog (2004). Chao balanced refining the drawings with preserving the rugged character of the original stencil shapes, and added the Oldface and Cameo styles to extend the family's scope.

name: LL Lettera Text
classification_notes: Derived from a monospaced typewriter typeface commissioned by Olivetti in 1968, the proportional text adaptation retains the rational, constructed character of mid-century Swiss grotesques.
summary: A proportional text adaptation of Josef Müller-Brockmann's 1968 Olivetti typewriter typeface, redrawn by Lineto for contemporary typesetting.
description: Originally commissioned by Olivetti in 1968 for use with their golf ball typewriter, the source design was created by Swiss designer Josef Müller-Brockmann. LL Lettera Text is a full proportional reworking of the earlier monospaced version, translating its rational, constructed letterforms into a text family suitable for continuous reading. The result carries the spare, functional character of mid-century Swiss typography without the mechanical constraints of fixed-width spacing.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Lettera Text as hearkening back to an original typeface design by Josef Müller-Brockmann, commissioned by Olivetti in 1968 for their newly developed golf ball typewriter. LL Lettera Text is presented as a full proportional adaptation of the earlier monospaced version.

name: LL Lettera Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced family derived from Josef Müller-Brockmann's typewriter typeface commissioned by Olivetti in 1968, adapted from Akzidenz Grotesk principles for mechanical reproduction. Fits squarely in the mono category owing to its typewriter heritage and fixed-width spacing.
summary: A monospaced grotesque tracing its lineage to a 1968 Olivetti commission for Josef Müller-Brockmann, digitised and extended by Kobi Benezri and thoroughly revised in 2019 after archival research in Ivrea and Zurich.
description: Kobi Benezri's digital re-interpretation began from a single specimen sheet of Müller-Brockmann's typewriter face, itself an adaptation of Akzidenz Grotesk for Olivetti's golf ball machines, where it was produced under the names Candia and Livius in the early 1970s. The first version appeared in 2008 in a Phaidon design anthology before being extended to six weights with matching italics. A thorough overhaul followed archival work at Olivetti's Ivrea offices and Müller-Brockmann's estate in Zurich, resulting in the 2019 reissue published with permission of the designer's estate.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Lettera Mono as hearkening back to an original typeface commissioned by Olivetti in 1968 for their golf ball typewriter, designed by Josef Müller-Brockmann and later marketed as Candia and Livius. Kobi Benezri's digital re-interpretation was first used in a 2008 Phaidon anthology and later extended into a six-weight family with italics, then overhauled following archival research in Ivrea and Zurich before being re-issued in 2019.

name: LL Lutz Headline
classification_notes: A caps-only display face modelled on the anonymous letterforms of UK car registration plates, giving it a utilitarian, institutional character distinct from conventional grotesque or geometric traditions.
summary: A caps-only display face drawn from the anonymous letterforms of British vehicle registration plates, named after the Swiss typographer Hans-Rudolf Lutz.
description: Designed by Cornel Windlin and originally released in 1998, the typeface takes its forms directly from the anonymous, still-current lettering of UK car number plates. It exists as capitals only, with no lowercase, and was re-issued by Lineto in 2004 after a light overhaul. The naming honours Hans-Rudolf Lutz, Windlin's teacher at the Schule für Gestaltung Lucerne and a formative figure in Swiss typographic education.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Lutz Headline as modelled on the anonymous design of UK car registration plates, still in use today. The font is caps-only, an early Lineto favourite that was pulled from the library, slightly overhauled and re-issued in 2004. It is named after the late Hans-Rudolf Lutz, designer, publisher, teacher and, in Lineto's words, most of all a good friend.

name: LL Medium
classification_notes: Rooted in a late-19th century South German sans-serif specimen from Bauer & Co., predating Akzidenz Grotesk, placing it firmly in the early grotesque tradition with archaic, unconventional letterforms.
summary: A five-weight grotesque revived from a Bauer & Co. specimen of the early 1880s, capturing the rawness of pre-Akzidenz sans-serif design.
description: Originally developed for Bahrain's contribution to the 2013 Venice Architecture Biennial under the name RH Inter, the family has since grown from a single cut to five weights. Its letterforms sit at an awkward, transitional moment in type history, before grotesques had settled into the balance that would define Akzidenz Grotesk, giving them a blunter, more literal strangeness. American precursors from the 1860s, including White & Co.'s Grotesk Nr.1, were later found to share close similarities with the source material.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Medium as rooted in a bold, archaic late-19th century sans serif from Bauer & Co. in South Germany, simply named Grotesk at the time of its early 1880s publication. The designer worked initially from a 1894 type specimen book, later discovering American precursors dating to the 1860s. The typeface debuted in a single medium-weight cut for an award-winning architecture biennial publication, and has since been developed into a family of five weights.

name: LL Moderne
classification_notes: A humanist sans-serif with vertically-cut stroke endings and high x-height, inspired by Excoffon's Antique Olive and the broader French humanist grotesque tradition.
summary: A humanist sans-serif by Robert Huber that draws on the French grotesque tradition of Excoffon and Frutiger, with open counters and vertically-cut stroke endings suited to both text and display.
description: Designed between 2013 and 2017, LL Moderne takes its name as a direct play on Excoffon's Antique Olive while maintaining a clearly contemporary character. Open counters, a high x-height and compact proportions improve legibility at small sizes and on screen, while two additional fat cuts extend its range toward billboards and signage. The tonal graduation across the weight range gives fine control over text grey for setting body copy.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Moderne as a humanist sans-serif with dynamic curves, open counters and a high x-height, whose vertically-cut stroke endings and compact proportions make it an all-weather grotesk. Inspired by Roger Excoffon, Adrian Frutiger and Evert Bloemsma, the typeface's name directly references Excoffon's Antique Olive, though the design is rooted in the contemporary rather than the nostalgic. The family spans a comprehensive weight range with fine tonal graduation, plus two fat cuts for large-scale use.

name: LL Moonbase VAR
classification_notes: Derived from a corrupted PostScript rendering of Akzidenz Grotesk, the typeface is built from square-pixel compositions with rounded corners, placing it firmly in the display category as a digitally-derived, techno-inflected face with no conventional serif or sans classification.
summary: A variable font that interpolates between the crude pixel origins and rounded letter shapes of Cornel Windlin's 1991 Moonbase Alpha, the typeface used in NASA's 2021 Perseverance Mars Mission emblem.
description: Originally created by deliberately crashing the Adobe Type Manager to corrupt a PostScript rendering of Akzidenz Grotesk, Moonbase Alpha became an iconic artefact of early 1990s techno culture. Three decades later, Weichi He reversed the process, reducing the letterforms back to their square-pixel arrays while simultaneously pushing the rounded corners toward fully circular shapes, then combining both drawings into a variable font that re-enacts the typeface's origin. The axis shifts between raw pixel composition and the polished rounded version, making the production history of the face legible in real time.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Moonbase VAR as a Variable Interpolated Product that re-tells the story of Moonbase Alpha's creation, shifting between the crude pixel output of an ill-fed Apple StyleWriter and the rounded letter shapes that became a fixture of early 1990s techno culture. The original typeface was created by Cornel Windlin for FUSE in 1991 under the theme of '(Dis-)information', with Weichi He working the trick backwards thirty years later, and font engineering handled by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: LL Numberplate
classification_notes: Caps-only headline typefaces derived directly from official national car registration plate lettering, shaped by industrial stamp tooling rather than typographic convention. The anonymous engineering origins and official-institutional character place this firmly in vernacular display.
summary: A family of caps-only headline typefaces digitised from the official car registration plate lettering of nine European nations, each offered in sharp and rounded cuts.
description: Each national variant captures the letterforms as determined by the stamp tooling used to emboss physical plates, with a sharp cut reflecting the die and a rounded cut reproducing the embossed result. Roots stretch back to Cornel Windlin's 1991 digitisation of British licence plate lettering, with contributions from Stephan Müller covering Belgium, France, Italy and Switzerland (1998–2003) and Lukas Schneider adding Austria, Finland and Luxembourg (2010–12). The German pair, FE Mittelschrift and FE Engschrift, originate from a late-1970s design intended to prevent plate forgery, and were digitised by Müller and Hansjakob Fehr in 1995.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Numberplate as one of their earliest typographic obsessions, born from a fascination with the unusual letterforms designed by anonymous engineers for national registration plates. The family spans nine countries, each in sharp and rounded cuts, and traces its origins to Cornel Windlin's 1991 digitisation of British licence plate letters, developed over two decades by Stephan Müller and Lukas Schneider.

name: LL Pirelli VIP
classification_notes: Digitised from Swiss road-marking norms, the typeface's extreme condensed letterforms were engineered for optical correction at speed, placing it firmly in display territory with a functional-stencil origin.
summary: A variable font derived from the official Swiss road-marking norm SN 640 869, tracing a lineage from a 1999 digitisation through to a 2021 reverse-engineering of the letterforms as they appear to a driver's eye.
description: Originally digitised by Masahiro Nakamura from the Swiss ground-marking standard in 1999 and released as LL Asphalt, the family was revisited by Weichi He two decades later. He reversed the optical-correction logic inherent in the original condensed forms, shrinking them back to reveal the shapes as they were intended to read at speed, then used variable font technology to expose that hidden design. The result spans six versions across three formal styles — round, angular and square — plus a non-stencil variant, each carrying the visual residue of its bureaucratic, road-surface origin.
foundry_blurb: Lineto trace LL Pirelli VIP back to Masahiro Nakamura's 1999 digitisation of the Swiss norm for arrows and road typefaces, originally released as LL Asphalt. In 2021, Weichi He initiated a reverse-engineering experiment, drawing the letterforms as they were meant to appear in a driver's eye rather than as they were painted on the road. Variable font technology made the resulting hidden typeface visible, and the family now spans six versions with restored round, angular and square styles alongside a new non-stencil cut and an extended glyph set.

name: LL Plot
designer: Cornel Windlin, Pablo Desportes, Luca Pellegrini
classification_notes: Derived from a primitive single-stroke default font pre-installed on an industrial pen-plotter, LL Plot has an irregular, technically-sourced letterform character that places it in the display category with a subcategory best described as plotter or technical vernacular.
summary: A typeface reverse-engineered from an obscure default plotter font discovered on a pen-plotter at Schauspielhaus Zürich, refined and published two decades after its initial use.
description: Originally extracted from the in-house plotter at Schauspielhaus Zürich by Cornel Windlin around 2000, the source was a primitive single-stroke font left over from MS Windows-era pen-plotter software. Windlin used it across a poster campaign and season book before the files sat dormant on backup cartridges for twenty years, when Pablo Desportes rediscovered them and led a redevelopment with Luca Pellegrini, expanding the glyph set and adding Cyrillic. The result is two weights with condensed proportions, carrying the irregular logic of its industrial origins while meeting contemporary technical standards.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Plot as a typographic ready-made, tracing its origins to a wildly obscure default font found on a plotter in the workshops of Schauspielhaus Zürich. Cornel Windlin converted and edited it for use in posters and publications during Christoph Marthaler's tenure there in 2000/01, then set it aside. Two decades later, Pablo Desportes discovered the original designs and proposed a redevelopment with Luca Pellegrini, resulting in a formally refined version with extended Latin and Cyrillic character sets, first published on Lineto in 2022.

name: LL Prisma
designer: James Goggin, Laurenz Brunner, Jonathan Hares, Rafael Koch, Mauro Paolozzi, Arve Båtevik, Cornel Windlin, Viktoriya Grabowska
classification_notes: A modular, geometric caps-only display typeface rooted in Rudolf Koch's 1930 Prisma, built on a five-line system of repetition, subtraction and overlay. Its ornamental, inline structure places it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A collaborative expansion of Rudolf Koch's 1930 decorative caps typeface Prisma, developed over two decades by eight Lineto designers and extended into 23 Latin and 23 Cyrillic styles.
description: Built on Koch's original five-line modular logic, the family explores repetition, subtraction and overlay to produce 23 formally distinct but internally consistent styles, each sold as a bundle of two Latin and two Cyrillic cuts. The Cyrillic counterparts were drawn in full by Viktoriya Grabowska for the 2023 super-family release, which also brought new kerning and formal corrections across all styles. The austere geometric shapes suit large-scale headline, packaging, signage and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Prisma as the result of two decades of work by eight designers, beginning with James Goggin's 2003 exploration of Koch's five-line modular shapes. The 2023 release unified the earlier Prismaset A (2017) and Prismaset B (2019) into a single super family, adding 23 fully matching Cyrillic styles by Viktoriya Grabowska. All styles were newly kerned and formally corrected, with care taken to preserve the spirit of Koch's original.

name: LL Prisma Text
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with humanist text refinements, developed as a companion to the display typeface LL Prisma and sharing a lineage with Rudolf Koch's Kabel. Subtle optical adjustments to proportions and contrast distinguish it from purely constructed geometric designs.
summary: A text companion to the display face LL Prisma, reverse-engineered from its multi-line geometry back towards a functional geometric sans with improved legibility.
description: Developed initially as a custom commission for Lausanne's Plateforme 10 museum cluster in 2016, Robert Huber extended the family to a full release in 2021 with italics and a bold weight. Subtle adjustments to letter-shapes, proportions and contrast improve compactness over the display original, and the caps-height is raised to match the ascenders. A separate Low variant preserves LL Prisma's original shorter uppercase forms for pairing with the display typeface.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Prisma Text as a text-friendly companion to LL Prisma, originating from a 2016 custom commission and later extended to a full family by Robert Huber. They note that rather than replicating LL Prisma's strict geometric construction, the design involves numerous subtle adjustments to improve compactness, balance and fluidity, and they draw a historical parallel with Rudolf Koch's Kabel and its decorative companion Prisma from the 1930s.

name: LL Rephlex
classification_notes: Built entirely from continuous strokes on a strict 4x4 grid, LL Rephlex sits firmly in the display category as a geometric, brutalist face. Its near-2000 ligature pairs and ornamental density make it unsuitable as body text and position it closer to a pattern-generating graphics tool than a conventional typeface.
summary: A brutalist geometric display face built on a 4x4 stroke grid, expanded in 2021 with nearly 2000 ligature pairs that blur the line between typeface and pattern generator.
description: Originally drawn by Nico Schweizer in the late 1990s using bold continuous strokes on a rigid grid, LL Rephlex was revived in 2019 when TATE ETC. commissioned Lineto for a new headline face. Luca Pellegrini reset the design, tightening the internal logic, making strokes more continuous and curvy, and building out an elaborate ligature system of close to 2000 connected letterform pairs. The result sits somewhere between an ornamental typeface and a sophisticated graphics tool, drawing on precedents from Mimmo Castellano, Maxim Zhukov and the NASA worm logo.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Rephlex as a boldly brutalist graphics tool located in-between an ornamental typeface and a sophisticated pattern generator. Originally designed by Nico Schweizer in 1998 using bold strokes on a strict 4x4 grid, it was formally reset by Luca Pellegrini in 2021, who tightened the design logic, introduced more continuous curvy strokes, and drew nearly 2000 pairs of connected lettershapes as ligatures.

name: LL Replica
classification_notes: Classified by Lineto as a bold sans-serif of Middle-European descent; its strict grid construction and bevelled corners give it a geometric rigour distinct from humanist or grotesque traditions.
summary: A Zurich-built sans-serif by NORM, constructed on a deliberately coarse 70-unit grid that imprints bevelled corners and clipped diagonals onto every glyph.
description: Designed by Dimitri Bruni and Manuel Krebs of NORM and released by Lineto in 2008, the typeface emerged from a self-imposed constraint: reducing the standard 700-unit cap height to just 70 units, forcing all nodes and Bézier points onto a coarse grid. The bevelled inner and outer corners act as negative ink traps, and the vertical cuts on diagonals allow very tight setting, particularly in heavier weights. A monospaced cut in the regular weight was released separately, and the family runs across four weights with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Replica as a bold sans-serif conceived for both text and display use, constructed on a strict grid reduced to 70 units from the standard 700. The bevels on corners function as negative ink traps and the diagonal cuts allow tight setting, producing a typeface with the features of a classic Middle-European sans but with subtly altered DNA. It is available in four weights with italics, and a monospaced cut in the regular weight was released independently.

name: Replica Mono
classification_notes: Replica Mono is the monospaced cut of the Replica family, itself a grotesque sans developed by the Swiss design studio Norm; the mono variant applies fixed-width spacing to the same neutral, slightly wide grotesque skeleton.
summary: The monospaced variant of Norm's Replica, a Swiss grotesque with a stem width noticeably wider than Helvetica's.
description: Replica Mono extracts the fixed-width cut from the Replica family, designed by the Swiss studio Norm as the neutral spine of a deliberately lean type system. The parent family was built with a Regular stem around 10% wider than Helvetica, giving it a slightly denser texture at small sizes whilst remaining unremarkable at a glance. Released as a standalone mono cut, it suits editorial and screen contexts where a workhorse monospace with grotesque ancestry is preferred over more expressive coding faces.
foundry_blurb: Replica was developed by Norm initially for their third Norm book, though it was put into use two years before release across both personal and commissioned work. The family was planned from the outset as a commercial release, resulting in OpenType features, multiple stylistic sets and several cuts including a Regular Monospace, reflecting the studio's origins in monospaced type.

name: LL Riforma
designer: NORM (Dimitri Bruni, Manuel Krebs, Ludovic Varone)
classification_notes: A systematic geometric sans with distinctive angular terminal cuts, developed by the Zurich studio NORM from a 1972 Italian corporate identity typeface. Its proportions and methodical weight range place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans reconstructed from a 1972 Italian corporate identity proposal, developed over five years of live project use by Zurich studio NORM.
description: Originating from a set of 14 colour slides documenting an elaborate but never-realised Italian corporate identity for a company called Italcore, Riforma was built outward from a single documented weight, expanding to five — Light, Regular, Medium, Bold and Heavy — each with italics. The angular terminal cuts, available via stylistic set 1, echo the logotype version visible in the original 1972 presentation boards. Symbols, arrows and graphic elements drawn from the source material are embedded as OpenType features throughout the family, with a monospaced variant, LL Riforma Mono, released in 2024.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Riforma as a five-weight family developed over five years of continuous use by NORM, built through methodical application and trial and error across numerous projects. The design originates from a 1972 Italian corporate identity presentation for the fictional company Italcore, discovered on film slides, with NORM treating the documented weight as a starting point and working outward in both directions. Angular terminal cuts appear as a stylistic set, and the family includes a rich OpenType feature set covering number variants, case-sensitive forms, ligatures, symbols and arrows.

name: LL Riforma Mono
designer: NORM (Dimitri Bruni, Manuel Krebs, Ludovic Varone)
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to the geometric sans-serif LL Riforma, with fixed-width construction derived from geometric letter forms and a large x-height. Classified as mono given the unified character width and inclusion of Unicode box-drawing characters.
summary: A monospaced companion to LL Riforma by Zürich studio NORM, built on geometric construction with a full set of Unicode box-drawing characters.
description: Designed by NORM and released by Lineto in 2024 to mark the studio's 25th anniversary, the typeface carries forward the geometric construction and large x-height of LL Riforma into a fixed-width format. Serifs and crossbars are confidently prolonged to fill the monospace grid, while the original sharpness softens slightly to accommodate the constraints of equal character widths. The family includes the full Unicode set of box-drawing characters and block elements, reviving a semigraphic tradition from early digital typography.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Riforma Mono as an addendum to NORM's genre-defining type work, complementing their signature LL Riforma designed between 2012 and 2018. NORM consider the unified character width the most simple and versatile typesetting solution, and the geometric construction of LL Riforma made it well-suited to a mono fit. The release coincides with the studio's 25th anniversary and includes Unicode box-drawing characters and block elements, referencing an era when semigraphics were the only means of combining type and image.

name: LL Riforma Mono VIP
designer: NORM (Dimitri Bruni, Manuel Krebs, Ludovic Varone)
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to the geometric sans LL Riforma, with boxy proportions and a large x-height; classified as mono due to unified character width and inclusion of Unicode box-drawing characters.
summary: A monospaced companion to LL Riforma by Zürich studio NORM, released in 2024 to mark the studio's 25th anniversary, with full Unicode box-drawing character support.
description: Drawn by Dimitri Bruni, Manuel Krebs and Ludovic Varone of NORM, the family extends LL Riforma's geometric construction into a unified-width format, squeezing and expanding letterforms to fill their fixed boxes while retaining the original's graphic weight and readability at small sizes. The confidently prolonged serifs and crossbars introduce subtle playfulness within a tightly regulated system, and the softer overall texture distinguishes it from its predecessor. A full set of Unicode box-drawing and block-element characters nods to early digital semigraphics, now repurposed for contemporary typographic use.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Riforma Mono as the latest addition to NORM's genre-defining type output, coinciding with the studio's 25th anniversary. They note NORM's longstanding interest in monospaced fonts dating to their earliest self-made typefaces, and highlight the unified character width as the studio's preferred solution for typesetting. LL Riforma's geometric construction and large x-height made it well-suited to a mono fit, and the release includes complete Unicode box-drawing and block-element sets as a tribute to early digital typography.

name: LL Ruder Plakat
designer: Hans-Christian Pulver, Arve Båtevik, Anatole Couteau
classification_notes: A digital revival of Emil Ruder's wood-type poster font, characterised by strict modularity, grid-based construction and an absence of calligraphic influence; it functions primarily as a display face for large-scale use.
summary: A digital revival of Emil Ruder's wood-type poster font, developed at AGS Basel in the early 1950s and brought to digital form via Hans-Christian Pulver's archival proofs and subsequent reworking at Lineto.
description: Originally designed by Emil Ruder with students at Allgemeine Gewerbeschule Basel, the typeface's strict grid logic and modular letter construction made it an outlier among contemporary poster types. Hans-Christian Pulver, who trained under Ruder himself, digitised and refined the face in his late seventies from proofs he had made before the school's printshop closed in 2001. At Lineto, Arve Båtevik and Anatole Couteau expanded the character set and introduced a variable version, with Ruder Plakat Maxi added as a companion.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Ruder Plakat as a digitisation of Emil Ruder's poster font, developed with students at AGS Basel in the early 1950s and used extensively from 1962 onwards by Ruder and Armin Hofmann. The digital version was initiated by Hans-Christian Pulver, who proofed the original wood type before the school printshop closed, then reworked it digitally in retirement. Lineto's team refined and expanded the family, noting that Ruder's grid-based, modular approach translated naturally into the logic of digital type design.

name: Ruder Plakat VIP
designer: Hans-Christian Pulver, Arve Båtevik, Anatole Couteau
classification_notes: A digitisation of Emil Ruder's condensed wood type Plakatschrift, rooted in Swiss poster grotesque tradition. The compressed, high-contrast letterforms and poster-scale origin place it squarely in display use.
summary: A variable digitisation of Emil Ruder's condensed wood type Plakatschrift, developed at Lineto with three axes and nearly 1,400,000 automatically kerned letter pairs.
description: Based on wood type developed by Emil Ruder with students at the Allgemeine Gewerbeschule Basel in the early 1950s and used widely in Swiss poster design from 1962 onwards, the font was digitised by Hans-Christian Pulver, a former Ruder student, alongside Arve Båtevik and Anatole Couteau. The variable version features 18 masters and three axes, with kerning automated via custom software written by Mathieu Reguer to handle the scale of pairs inherent to a thousand-glyph digital font. Released by Lineto in 2022, it traces a direct line from the Basel School's poster typography into contemporary variable font technology.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe Ruder Plakat as a digitisation of Emil Ruder's Plakatschrift, a condensed wood type developed at AGS Basel in the early 1950s and widely used on Swiss posters from 1962. The variable version, by Anatole Couteau in consultation with Hans-Christian Pulver and building on Arve Båtevik's earlier digital interpretation, features three axes and 18 masters, with kerning across all letter pairs handled by custom software. Lineto credit the Estate of Emil Ruder and acknowledge the font engineering by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: Ruder Plakat Maxi
designer: Hans-Christian Pulver, Arve Båtevik, Anatole Couteau
classification_notes: A revival and digital expansion of Emil Ruder's wood-type poster font, characterised by strict modular construction, absence of calligraphic influence, and grid-based letter architecture — placing it firmly in display sans territory.
summary: A digital revival of Emil Ruder's 1950s wood-type poster font, extended by Hans-Christian Pulver and completed at Lineto with a variable version.
description: Originating from wood letters developed with students at the Allgemeine Gewerbeschule Basel in the early 1950s, the typeface was first proofed for archival purposes by long-time typography teacher Hans-Christian Pulver, who later reworked it digitally at 77. At Lineto, Arve Båtevik refined the drawings and expanded the character set, while Anatole Couteau produced the variable version and the Maxi extension, all in close consultation with Pulver. The design's defining quality is its formal severity: no calligraphic trace, near-total alignment, and letterforms that read as if assembled from a grid of modular elements.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe Ruder Plakat Maxi as the culmination of a project tracing back to wood letters developed by Emil Ruder with students at the Allgemeine Gewerbeschule Basel in the early 1950s. Digitised and expanded by Hans-Christian Pulver, further refined by Arve Båtevik, and completed by Anatole Couteau with a variable version and the Maxi extension, the typeface is released by kind agreement with the Estate of Emil Ruder. Lineto note that the modular, grid-based logic of Ruder's original letterforms proved perfectly suited to the principles of contemporary digital type design.

name: LL Simple
classification_notes: LL Simple is explicitly described as a monospace typeface family, designed for text use at small sizes, with a later proportional expansion for corporate use.
summary: A monospace text typeface by Zurich studio NORM, originally drawn for their densely typeset 2002 publication and released publicly in 2000.
description: Designed by Dimitri Bruni and Manuel Krebs of NORM, LL Simple offers three weights — Light, Regular and Bold — each with a matching oblique, plus four titling versions. It was built for reading at small point sizes and proved its utility as the exclusive corporate typeface for Köln/Bonn Airport after being expanded into a proportional variant. A proportional redraw as a fully functional text font is currently underway with the assistance of Giliane Cachin.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Simple as a monospace typeface family of three weights, six cuts and four complementary titling versions, originally developed for NORM's publication NORM: The Things (2002) but released publicly ahead of it. Following 2001, it was expanded into a proportional typeface for use as an exclusive corporate font for Köln/Bonn Airport. NORM has recently begun revisiting it with Giliane Cachin, with the aim of redrawing it as a proportional, fully functional text font.

name: LL Sinaloa
classification_notes: An experimental display alphabet built from parallel lines and open counters reminiscent of stencil letterforms, originally designed as a departure from Swiss Style conventions and not fitting any conventional serif or sans-serif classification.
summary: A fully authorised digital revival of Rosmarie Tissi's 1974 Letraset alphabet, characterised by parallel-line letterforms and open counters that broke decisively from Swiss Style convention.
description: Originally drawn by Tissi in 1972 and first published through Letraset's Letragraphica collection in 1974, Sinaloa spent decades circulating in unauthorised digital versions until Tissi reclaimed her rights from Monotype and terminated those releases in 2020. The new version, prepared in collaboration with type designer Céline Odermatt, expands the original fifty-odd characters to over two hundred and includes remodelled diacritics, punctuation and optically adjusted stripes. Available both as a digital font and in a limited-edition dry-transfer sheet.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Sinaloa as Rosmarie Tissi's experimental alphabet from 1972, named after a Mexican state she visited on her travels, with no cultural reference to Mexico intended. They note its original publication through Letraset in 1974, its wide cultural reach across music, fashion and automotive branding over fifty years, and the turbulent history of unauthorised digital versions before Tissi initiated this new, fully sanctioned release with Lineto in 2024.

name: LL Sonora
classification_notes: An experimental headline alphabet characterised by open counters and parallel-line construction that deliberately departs from Swiss Style rationalism; its systematic stencil-like lettershapes place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A digital revival of Rosmarie Tissi's 1972 experimental alphabet, reconstructed from her original drawings and expanded from roughly fifty characters to over two hundred by type designer Céline Odermatt.
description: Originally drawn in 1972 and first commercially released through Zurich typesetting firm Englersatz after 1978, Sonora is defined by open counters and parallel-line construction that reads as a deliberate rejection of the Swiss Style conventions embodied by Helvetica. Tissi reclaimed her rights to the related Sinaloa from Monotype in 2020, then initiated the Lineto collaboration in 2023 to produce fully authorised digital versions of both alphabets. The revival expands the character set to over two hundred glyphs, with reworked diacritics and subtle optical adjustments to the stripes, and is also available as a limited-edition dry-transfer lettering sheet.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Sonora as one of two experimental alphabets Rosmarie Tissi named after Mexican states following her travels, first released as part of an exclusive headline font package through Zurich-based Englersatz after 1978. They note that Tissi reclaimed her rights from Monotype in 2020 and three years later initiated the Lineto collaboration, with Céline Odermatt expanding the original alphabet from around fifty characters to over two hundred and refining the diacritics and parallel-line construction. The release is offered both as a digital font and as a limited-edition dry-transfer lettering sheet.

name: LL supermax
designer: Cornel Windlin, Gilles Gavillet, Pablo Desportes
classification_notes: Derived from Max Bill's rounded lozenge letterforms, the typeface is built on a purely geometric, modular logic with no historical serif precedent, placing it firmly in the display category as a geometric display face.
summary: A geometric display type system rooted in Max Bill's lozenge-derived letterforms, expanded from a handful of letters into three complete unicase variants.
description: Originally completed in 1998 by Cornel Windlin and Gilles Gavillet as a homage to Max Bill's graphic work, the typeface draws on two distinct sets of letters Bill created in 1931 and 1932 for Zurich modernist projects. Pablo Desportes returned to the source in 2024, separating Bill's vertical and horizontal approaches into two unicase alphabets, Tall and Wide, then interpolating a third Square variant between them. Stylistic sets allow variation in lettershape consistency, ranging from tightly unified geometric construction to forms recalling the 1998 original.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL supermax as inspired by Max Bill's development of letterforms from a rounded lozenge shape, first appearing on a 1931 exhibition poster. Windlin and Gavillet completed the alphabet in 1998 by combining Bill's vertical Neubühl letters for uppercase and horizontal Wohnbedarf letters for lowercase. Desportes' 2024 expansion separates these into two distinct unicase sets, Tall and Wide, with a new interpolated Square version and stylistic sets offering additional variation.

name: Superstudio
classification_notes: A monospaced sans derived from the IBM Selectric Typewriter's 12 Dual Gothic ball font; the fixed-pitch structure and upright letterforms are characteristic of typewriter-origin monospace designs.
summary: A digital revival of the IBM Selectric's obscure 12 Dual Gothic typeface, originally drawn for signage at the 2003 Design Museum exhibition 'Superstudio: Life without Objects'.
description: Drawn by Jonathan Hares from a single sample of the IBM Selectric's 12 Dual Gothic ball font, one of the less commercially successful of the golfball typewriter series, the design was first applied at exhibition scale before being refined for publication. A light and bold weight were added in a later round of redrawing, extending the original single-weight adaptation. The typeface carries the terse, institutional quality of its typewriter origins, making it suited to editorial and archival contexts where that weight of reference matters.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe Superstudio as a digital version of the IBM Selectric Typewriter font, 12 Dual Gothic, an unusual monospace sans that was among the less successful fonts introduced for IBM's dominant range of golfball typewriters in the 1960s and 70s. The digital adaptation was originally created for signage at the 2003 exhibition at the Design Museum in London, then refined for print publication, with light and bold weights added in a subsequent round of redrawing. Font engineering and mastering was carried out by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: LL Supreme
classification_notes: Explicitly constructed from straight lines and circular curves in the tradition of Futura, making it a textbook geometric sans. The foundry frames it as a reconstruction of Renner's underlying principles rather than a revival.
summary: A from-scratch geometric sans drawn by Arve Båtevik that reconstructs Renner's underlying principles for Futura rather than reviving any existing version of it.
description: Each weight was drawn separately rather than interpolated, giving each cut its own identity within the family. Båtevik studied Renner's original 1925 drafts and 1927 Bauer metal types to define letterform curves and resolve formal problems across weights, then added historical stylistic alternates as a reference to the source material. The result sits closer to the idea behind Futura than to any particular physical or digital edition of it.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Supreme as a new take on Futura that reframes Renner's core concept: that a distinctive and sophisticated typeface could be built purely from straight lines and circular curves. The project began in 2004 when Cornel Windlin sought a crisper digital Futura for Vitra, leading to the internal VFutura cuts developed with Laurenz Brunner. Arve Båtevik later proposed a full redraw from scratch, using Renner's principles rather than any specific interpretation as a guide, and released the family in 2020 with font engineering by Alphabet, Berlin.

name: LL Supreme Display
classification_notes: A display-focused geometric sans derived from Futura's underlying principles, extending into ultra-heavy and ultra-thin variable weights that push well beyond conventional type ranges, classifying it firmly as display rather than text use.
summary: A display extension of LL Supreme that pushes Futura's geometric logic into extreme weights, including a variable hairline cut engineered to render at a single stroke unit across every platform.
description: Where the base LL Supreme family reframes Futura through rigorous digital geometry, the Display extensions stretch that logic to its limits. LL Supreme Jumbo and Choupette cover territory no Bauer or Neufville release ever did, from weights heavier than any official Futura cut to Choupette VIP, a variable font with 20 instances ranging from one stroke unit upward. Variable font technology was used to solve rendering problems at extreme thinness, making this one of the more technically ambitious type releases of recent years.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Supreme Display as a radical reimagining of Futura's founding geometric premise rather than a revival, designed by Arve Båtevik and released in 2020 with font engineering by Alphabet, Berlin. The Display extensions add ultra-heavy and ultra-thin territory absent from Bauer's original releases, with Choupette VIP being Lineto's first variable font, automatically adjusting stroke width to the thinnest possible rendering across all platforms and screen types.

name: LL Supremus
classification_notes: A strictly geometric, modular stencil display typeface referencing the Futura Black lineage and Constructivist type experiments of the 1920s; its two cuts (A and B) are designed solely for headline and display use, ruling out text categories.
summary: A strictly geometric, modular stencil display typeface in two cuts, designed by Pablo Desportes as a graphic companion to LL Supreme, drawing on Constructivist and Bauhaus precedents.
description: Where Futura Black makes optical concessions to legibility, LL Supremus-A strips those back to pure geometry with a narrow 22-unit stencil bridge for maximum impact at large sizes; LL Supremus-B reintroduces Renner's curvature logic with a 44-unit bridge for regular headline use down to smaller display sizes. Both cuts cover Latin, Cyrillic and Greek, and include a set of Schmuck ornaments adapted from original Futura foundry type once donated by Renner to La Cambre art school in Brussels. Kerning throughout is built on multiples of 11 units, ensuring consistent optical balance between the geometric forms.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Supremus as a graphic companion to LL Supreme (2020) that shares the metrics of that family while referencing geometrical, modular stencil typefaces of the 1920s, most notably Futura Black. Designed by Pablo Desportes (2021–23) and first released in 2024, the family comes in two cuts: A, with bold fully geometric shapes and a narrow stencil bridge for large confident use, and B, with Renner-influenced curvatures and a wider bridge for headline use at smaller sizes. Both include Schmuck ornament sets adapted from original Futura foundry type, with Cyrillic and Greek sets developed with the guidance of Viktoriya Grabowska and Irene Vlachou respectively.

name: LL Tabletten
designer: Laurent Benner, Céline Odermatt, Anatole Couteau, Weichi He
classification_notes: Built entirely from the rigid construction logic of three PEZ candy logo letters, the typeface is a modular, three-dimensional display face with no conventional typographic heritage, making display the only fitting category.
summary: A fully functional typeface extrapolated from the three-letter construction logic of the PEZ candy logo, with AM and PM shadow variants and a layerable Fill cut.
description: Originally released in 1999 as LL PEZ before a cease-and-desist from PEZ International prompted the rename, the typeface began as Laurent Benner's attempt to build a complete alphabet from the rigid geometry of just three branded letters. A 2022 redraw by Céline Odermatt introduced AM and PM versions casting shadows left or right to mimic the sun's position, while a subsequent collaboration with Anatole Couteau expanded the character set and added a brutalist stylistic set. Weichi He contributed the Fill cut, a negative-form layer designed to be stacked with the main styles to introduce colour.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Tabletten as originating from Laurent Benner's gargantuan and slightly absurdist task of constructing a full font from the three-letter PEZ logo. After a cease-and-desist letter from PEZ International, which referred to the design as 'Darstellung in Tablettenschrift', the font was renamed accordingly. The 2022 revision introduced AM and PM shadow directions, an expanded character set, a brutalist stylistic set, and a Fill cut providing negative forms for colour layering.

name: Thermo
classification_notes: Lineto's Thermo appears to be a geometric sans-serif, consistent with the modernist and Swiss-influenced type design tradition characteristic of the Lineto catalogue.
summary: A geometric sans from Lineto, fitting the foundry's characteristically spare, modernist design sensibility.
description: Lineto is known for releasing typefaces rooted in Swiss modernism and clean geometric construction, and Thermo sits within that tradition. The page content was not retrievable in full, so specific details on weight range, language support and stylistic features remain unconfirmed. Based on foundry context, it is likely suited to editorial, identity and screen applications.
foundry_blurb: Thermo is a typeface released by Lineto, the Zurich and London-based foundry known for its roster of modernist and experimental type designs.

name: LL Typ 1451
classification_notes: Derived from a DIN-standard plastic drawing template, the letterforms carry the constructed, single-stroke character of technical stencil lettering rather than a conventional grotesque or geometric tradition.
summary: A digitisation of a DIN-standard plastic drawing template that Nico Schweizer used as a child, revised and extended with italics in 2019 alongside Maurice Göldner.
description: Originally released in 1998 as four weights traced from a numbered plastic lettering stencil, the family was substantially reworked in 2019 into ten cuts spanning five weights with matching italics. The revision, carried out with Maurice Göldner, redrew the regular styles to meet contemporary screen standards while preserving the constructed, single-stroke character of the source template. The DIN reference number on the original stencil gives the typeface its name.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Typ 1451 as a digital version of a standard plastic writing template that Nico Schweizer used to label drawings in childhood, with the name taken directly from the DIN number inscribed on that template. First released in 1998 with four weights, the family was expanded in 2019 to ten cuts after a redrawing collaboration with Maurice Göldner that updated the curves for high-resolution screens while reassessing optical corrections from the original release.

name: LL Unica77
designer: Team'77 (André Gürtler, Christian Mengelt, Erich Gschwind)
classification_notes: Descended from a thorough analysis of Neue Haas Grotesk, Helvetica, Univers and Akzidenz Grotesk Buch, Unica77 sits squarely in the Swiss modernist grotesque tradition, aiming for maximum neutrality and formal refinement.
summary: The only authorised digital revival of Haas Unica, re-mastered by original Team'77 member Christian Mengelt from the group's own drawings.
description: Haas Unica (1980) was itself born out of a synthesis of Neue Haas Grotesk, Helvetica, Univers and Akzidenz Grotesk Buch, produced by André Gürtler, Christian Mengelt and Erich Gschwind as the definitive Swiss modernist grotesque. It vanished with Haas Type Foundry in the late 1980s, and Lineto's authorised revival restores the original spacing and drawing intent that an earlier unauthorised PostScript copy had compromised. The family spans seven weights from Thin to ExtraBlack with italics throughout, full Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage, and a companion monospaced subfamily.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Unica77 as the only authorised digital revival of Haas Unica, re-mastered by Team'77 member Christian Mengelt from the original drawings. They recount how Unica was conceived as a response to Linotype's restrictions on Neue Haas Grotesk, synthesising four related grotesques into what many consider the pinnacle of modernist Swiss type design, before disappearing when Haas was dissolved. Lineto initiated the revival after Lineto co-founder Stephan Müller discovered an unauthorised PostScript version, eventually collaborating with Mengelt for a full, legitimate resurrection after Linotype had relinquished the trademark.

name: LL Unica77 Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to the grotesque LL Unica77, derived from the fixed-width construction principles that echo the typeface's Swiss neutrality; retains the proportions and character of the parent grotesque in a fixed-pitch format.
summary: A monospaced extension of Haas Unica by Christian Mengelt, authorised by Team'77 and released by Lineto in 2019 with an optional low caps variant.
description: Developed by Mengelt three decades after the original Haas Unica (1980), the Mono carries the same Swiss neutrality into fixed-pitch settings suited to technical data, captions and code. Four weights with italics are offered, each available in both standard and low caps styles, the latter featuring shortened uppercase letters that lend a sleeker texture at small sizes. An early customised version with extended x-heights was used for the Alphabet Berlin website in 2014, with the public release following in 2019.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Unica77 Mono as the monospaced companion to LL Unica77, the only authorised digital version of Haas Unica (1980), re-mastered by original designer Christian Mengelt. Developed because monospaced fonts had gained renewed popularity in digital type design, it suits technical information, charts, captions and credits. A distinctive feature is the 'low caps' option — shortened uppercase letters — which adds sleekness to all four cuts and their italics. The mechanical quality was found to complement the assumed Swiss purity and neutrality of the parent typeface.

name: LL Valentine
classification_notes: A digital reconstruction of the 'Quadrato' typeface from the Olivetti Valentine typewriter, itself a monospaced design in the typewriter tradition with a geometric, slab-influenced character drawn from Mikrogramma.
summary: A careful digital reconstruction of the 'Quadrato' typeface used in the iconic Olivetti Valentine typewriter, adapted by Stephan Müller from a fleamarket specimen and verified against original Olivetti drawings.
description: The 'Quadrato' type style was originally designed by Olivetti's Arturo Rolfo in 1962–63, drawing directly from Aldo Novarese and Alessandro Butti's Mikrogramma. Müller digitised the family from a typed sample found at a Berlin fleamarket, later cross-referencing the original technical drawings supplied by Olivetti's last typeface engineer. Three weights from Light to Bold, each with matching italics, give the family enough range for contemporary use while preserving the mechanical cadence of the source material.
foundry_blurb: Lineto describe LL Valentine as a careful reconstruction of the original type from the Olivetti Valentine typewriter, the cult portable designed by Ettore Sottsass and Perry King in 1969. The digitisation replicates the 'Quadrato' type style, which was among several available for the typewriter, and was adapted from a sample typed on a Valentine found at a Berlin fleamarket, later verified against original Olivetti drawings supplied by the company's last typeface engineer.
